The proxy statement says the company has an independent Board Chair, 11 of 12 director nominees are independent, all committee members are independent, and independent directors hold executive sessions after every regular Board meeting.
points: The Board's stated oversight areas include safety and quality, development programs, succession planning, human capital, cybersecurity, and sustainability risks.
The proxy says Boeing's Board and management are focused on strengthening safety and quality, and the company maintains a dedicated Safety & Quality website.
The independent Board Chair and fully independent committees provide a governance structure for overseeing management during a period that includes leadership changes and major portfolio actions.

types.summary: Boeing operates at a scale few industrial companies can match, with FY2025 revenue of $77.794 billion and R&D expense of $3.377 billion. The 10-K references major airplane programs including 737, 777X, and 787, indicating a complex product base that requires significant engineering, production, certification, and capital resources.

Boeing's business is not a single-product aircraft operation; its 10-K identifies Commercial Airplanes, Defense, Space & Security, and Global Services as operating segments. That breadth gives investors exposure to aircraft production, defense and space activities, and services, though the packet also shows FY2025 net income was negative $2.222 billion.

types.type: Vertical integration from Spirit AeroSystems acquisition
types.summary: Boeing completed the acquisition of Spirit AeroSystems on December 8, 2025, a portfolio move tied to its aircraft manufacturing base. The packet does not quantify cost savings or production benefits, but the acquisition is a concrete structural change in how Boeing controls parts of its aerospace production chain.

Boeing also completed a 2025 divestiture of portions of its digital aviation solutions business, showing management is reshaping the portfolio rather than simply adding assets. This is a narrower moat support point than aircraft programs or segment breadth, but it may help concentrate resources on Boeing's core aerospace operations.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Bank of America is led by a long-tenured CEO, Brian T. Moynihan, who has been CEO and a director since January 2010 and Chair since October 2014. The senior team also changed in 2025, with Dean C. Athanasia and James P. DeMare becoming Co-Presidents and Alastair M. Borthwick becoming CFO.
points: Brian T. Moynihan has led the company as Chief Executive Officer since January 2010 and also serves as Chair, giving him long operating history across credit, markets, and regulatory cycles.
Dean C. Athanasia and James P. DeMare became Co-Presidents in September 2025 after running Regional Banking and Global Markets, respectively, bringing segment-level experience into top leadership.
Alastair M. Borthwick became Chief Financial Officer in September 2025, while Geoffrey S. Greener has served as Chief Risk Officer since April 2014, providing continuity in risk leadership.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Bank of America manages a very large balance sheet, with FY2025 stockholders' equity of $273.197 billion, long-term debt of $283.279 billion, and operating cash flow of $44.982 billion. The packet supports scale and profitability, but it does not provide enough detail on buybacks, dividends, or acquisition discipline to grade capital allocation as strong.
points: For FY2025, Bank of America reported $102.769 billion of revenue, $26.305 billion of net income, and $44.982 billion of operating cash flow, showing large internal earnings generation.
At December 31, 2025, the company reported book value per share of $38.44 and tangible book value per share of $28.73 across 7.212464 billion ending common shares.
The proxy appendix reconciled 2025 average shareholders' equity of $298.474 billion to average tangible common shareholders' equity of $204.372 billion after specified deductions.
Long-term debt was $283.279 billion at FY2025, slightly above reported stockholders' equity of $273.197 billion, making funding, asset quality, and interest-rate management central allocation issues.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The proxy states that the Board focuses executive compensation on long-term shareholder value creation, performance, appropriate risk-taking, and pay-for-performance principles. Alignment is supported by shareholder-approved equity plans, but the packet does not include insider ownership levels, realized pay outcomes, or total capital-return history.
points: The Lead Independent Director's proxy letter says executive compensation is designed around long-term shareholder value creation, performance, appropriate risk-taking, and pay-for-performance principles.
At December 31, 2025, 276,043,362 shares were issuable under outstanding options, warrants, and rights under shareholder-approved equity compensation plans.
Bank of America reported 198,266,120 shares remaining for future issuance under shareholder-approved equity plans and no equity compensation plans not approved by shareholders.
The company has an Insider Trading Policy covering directors, officers, employees, and the company itself for purchases, sales, and other dispositions of Bank of America securities.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Bank of America provides detailed public reporting through its Form 10-K, SEC company facts, XBRL tags, and proxy appendix. The disclosures include segment-like business categories, fee income categories, derivative exposures, risk tags, tangible equity reconciliations, controls conclusions, and a specific sanctions-related transaction disclosure.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K XBRL data identifies loan categories including residential mortgage, junior-lien home equity, credit card and other unsecured consumer, vehicle, and commercial portfolios.

The company discloses fee and noninterest income categories such as interchange fees, deposit-related service charges, asset management fees, brokerage fees, underwriting income, and advisory services.
Bank of America separately reported 2025 sales and trading revenue of $20.871 billion, net DVA losses of $35 million, and sales and trading revenue excluding net DVA of $20.906 billion.

The proxy appendix reconciles average shareholders' equity to average tangible common shareholders' equity, including deductions for goodwill, intangible assets, deferred tax liabilities, and preferred stock.
The 2025 10-K disclosed one Q4 authorized OFAC-related wire payment totaling $3,254 and stated there was no measurable gross revenue or net profit except nominal processing fees.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance has formal independent oversight through Lead Independent Director Lionel L. Nowell III and regular executive sessions of independent directors. The structure also concentrates authority because Brian T. Moynihan is both Chair and CEO, making the lead independent director and risk oversight especially important for shareholders.
points: Lionel L. Nowell III is identified as Lead Independent Director and stated that independent directors met regularly in executive session without management present.
The Lead Independent Director said independent directors engage with management on performance, risk management, strategic direction, and corporate culture.
Brian T. Moynihan serves as both Chair and Chief Executive Officer, so board-level independent oversight is an important counterweight within the governance structure.
The company's disclosed risk areas include interest-rate risk, foreign-currency risk, commodity price risk, mortgage activity risk, and credit risk on loans.
As of the end of 2025, the CEO and CFO concluded disclosure controls and procedures were effective, with no reported Q4 internal-control changes that materially affected financial reporting.

types.summary: Bank of America has scale that few financial institutions can replicate, with FY2025 revenue of $102.769 billion, net income of $26.305 billion, and stockholders' equity of $273.197 billion. That scale supports a broad banking and non-banking platform serving people, companies, and institutions across multiple financial needs.

The company earns from many categories, including consumer loans, residential mortgages, credit cards, vehicle loans, commercial portfolios, interchange fees, deposit service charges, asset management fees, brokerage fees, underwriting, syndication, and advisory services. This breadth reduces dependence on a single product line, though it also creates complexity and multiple risk exposures.

Bank of America says it goes to market through eight lines of business designed for specific client groups while collaborating through shared insights, coordinated strategies, and a seamless client experience. The packet supports a broad operating model, but it does not quantify cross-sell rates, retention, customer counts, or segment market share.

The company reported 2025 sales and trading revenue excluding net DVA of $20.906 billion and discloses activity across interest rate, foreign exchange, equity, commodity, and credit derivatives. This points to a sizable institutional markets capability, but trading results remain exposed to market, derivative, and risk-management conditions.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: BlackRock is led by Laurence D. Fink, who signed the 2026 proxy as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer and is identified as the chief operating decision maker in the 10-K. The company is managed as one asset management business, with decisions based on consolidated revenue, operating income, and net income attributable to BlackRock.
points: Laurence D. Fink serves as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, and the 10-K identifies the Chairman and CEO as the company's chief operating decision maker.
Management runs BlackRock as one asset management operating segment, rather than as separately managed product divisions, using consolidated financial information for operating decisions.
The leadership team is overseeing a broad platform spanning active, private markets, index, cash management, whole-portfolio solutions, and Aladdin-supported technology services.
BlackRock ended 2025 with $14 trillion in assets under management, record $698 billion of net inflows, and approximately 24,900 employees in more than 30 countries.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: BlackRock's capital allocation framework is tied to consolidated total revenue, operating income, and net income attributable to BlackRock, which management also uses for compensation and cost management. In 2025, the company combined platform investment with a record $5.0 billion returned to shareholders through dividends and repurchases.
points: The 10-K says management uses total revenue, operating income, and net income attributable to BlackRock for capital allocation, annual and long-term compensation, and cost control.
BlackRock returned a record $5.0 billion to shareholders in 2025 through dividends and share repurchases, according to the proxy statement.
The company repurchased 1.6 million shares and share equivalents for about $1.6 billion in 2025, with about 2.2 million shares still authorized at year-end.
BlackRock paid $20.84 per share, or $3.3 billion, in cash dividends during 2025, and the board approved a $5.73 quarterly dividend for March 2026.
The proxy says the board targets a 40% to 50% dividend payout ratio and uses a consistent share repurchase program as part of capital returns.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Shareholder alignment is supported by a stated dividend payout target, ongoing repurchases, and executive compensation processes that reference the same consolidated metrics used to run the business. The packet does not provide insider ownership levels, but it does show meaningful cash returns and a non-binding shareholder vote on named executive officer pay.
points: BlackRock's board targets a 40% to 50% dividend payout ratio, creating a stated framework for returning a portion of earnings to shareholders.
Shareholders are asked to approve named executive officer compensation in a non-binding advisory vote, giving investors a recurring say-on-pay mechanism.
Management uses total revenue, operating income, and net income attributable to BlackRock when determining annual and long-term compensation, according to the 10-K.
The 2025 capital return mix included both $3.3 billion of dividends and about $1.6 billion of repurchases, rather than relying on only one return channel.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: BlackRock discloses that it operates in one asset management operating segment, so investors receive consolidated rather than multi-segment operating reporting. The company does provide useful operating context through disclosed assets under management, net inflows, organic base fee growth, technology services annual contract value growth, and SEC-reported financial figures.
points: The 10-K says BlackRock operates in one asset management operating segment, and that management directs the company as one business using consolidated financial information.
The company identifies total revenue, operating income, and net income attributable to BlackRock as the main measures used by the chief operating decision maker.
The proxy reported 2025 operating indicators including $14 trillion of assets under management, $698 billion of net inflows, 9% organic base fee growth, and 16% technology services ACV growth.
SEC company facts provide FY2025 revenue of $17.859 billion, net income of $5.502 billion, operating cash flow of $4.165 billion, and stockholders' equity of $47.495 billion.

The single-segment structure simplifies reporting but gives investors less product-level segment detail than a business reporting separate active, index, private markets, cash, and technology segments.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance disclosures include board-level capital return policy, auditor ratification, executive compensation voting, and a proposed subsidiary charter amendment. Investors should also watch regulated-subsidiary constraints, including net capital requirements across jurisdictions and OCC capital requirements at BlackRock Institutional Trust Company, N.A.
points: The proxy says the board approved a quarterly dividend of $5.73 per share for March 2026 and targets a 40% to 50% dividend payout ratio.
BlackRock submits Deloitte LLP's appointment as independent registered public accounting firm for fiscal 2026 to shareholders for ratification.
The proxy proposes amending BlackRock Finance, Inc.'s certificate of incorporation to remove a pass-through voting provision described as highly unusual for a consolidated subsidiary.
The 10-K says certain regulated subsidiaries must maintain net capital across multiple jurisdictions and may face restrictions moving cash between jurisdictions and to parent entities.

BlackRock Institutional Trust Company, N.A. is subject to OCC regulatory capital requirements, and failure to meet minimum requirements could directly materially affect consolidated financial statements.

types.summary: BlackRock's scale is unusually large for an asset manager, with the proxy reporting a new assets under management high of $14 trillion at the end of 2025. That scale sits across active, private markets, index, and cash management strategies, allowing the company to serve clients through multiple asset classes and whole-portfolio solutions.

BlackRock's investment platform is supported by Aladdin, its technology and risk management system, and the company offers technology services to institutional and wealth management clients. Technology services annual contract value grew 16% in 2025, showing that the platform is not only internal infrastructure but also a client-facing service line.

BlackRock describes a platform that includes alpha-seeking active strategies, private markets, index strategies, cash management, whole-portfolio solutions, and technology services. The breadth matters because 2025 net inflows reached a record $698 billion and reflected 9% organic base fee growth, indicating client demand across the platform.

BlackRock operates through regulated subsidiaries in multiple jurisdictions, and the 10-K says certain entities must maintain net capital and may face limits on cash transfers. These requirements can add complexity for competitors, but they also create operating constraints, including OCC capital requirements at BlackRock Institutional Trust Company, N.A.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Berkshire completed a major CEO transition with Gregory E. Abel becoming Chief Executive Officer on January 1, 2026, after serving as Vice Chairman - Non-Insurance Operations and previously leading Berkshire Hathaway Energy. The leadership structure still includes Warren E. Buffett as Chairman and Ajit Jain overseeing insurance operations, preserving experienced oversight across Berkshire's two most important management domains.
points: Gregory E. Abel brings direct Berkshire operating experience: he was Vice Chairman - Non-Insurance Operations from 2018 to 2025 and CEO of Berkshire Hathaway Energy from 2008 to 2018.
Warren E. Buffett served as CEO from 1970 through 2025 and continues as Chairman, while also holding about 30.0% of voting interest and 13.7% of economic interest.
Ajit Jain has been Vice Chairman - Insurance Operations and a director since 2018, with 39 years managing Berkshire reinsurance operations and pricing large, complex risks.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Berkshire's capital allocation is centered on large-scale liquidity, subsidiaries, and selective ownership changes rather than routine buybacks. At December 31, 2025, the Parent Company held $14.627 billion of cash and $112.811 billion of U.S. Treasury Bills, while also owning $604.100 billion of investments in and advances to consolidated subsidiaries.
points: In 2025, the Parent Company purchased $170.445 billion of U.S. Treasury Bills and had $166.935 billion of maturities, sales of Treasury Bills, and other related items.
Berkshire did not acquire treasury stock in 2025, after repurchasing $2.918 billion in 2024 and $9.171 billion in 2023, showing buybacks are not automatic.
The company increased subsidiary control in recent years, including acquiring the remaining Pilot noncontrolling interests for $2.6 billion and making BHE wholly owned after 2024 transactions.
Berkshire Hathaway Energy expanded its Cove Point LNG economic interest from 25% to 75% in 2023 through a $3.3 billion purchase of an additional limited partner interest.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Shareholder alignment is unusually tied to ownership and board selection criteria rather than equity incentive formulas described in the packet. Buffett remains Chairman with approximately 30.0% voting interest and 13.7% economic interest, and the Governance Committee seeks directors with an owner-oriented attitude and a significant Berkshire share investment relative to their resources for at least three years.
points: Warren E. Buffett's ownership gives him approximately 30.0% of voting interest and 13.7% of economic interest, creating substantial economic exposure alongside voting influence.

The Governance Committee's director criteria specifically include an owner-oriented attitude, a deep genuine interest in Berkshire, and meaningful Berkshire share ownership relative to personal resources.
Non-employee director pay is modest by public-company standards in the packet: $900 per in-person board meeting, $300 per telephone meeting, and $1,000 quarterly for Audit Committee service.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Berkshire's investor communication in the packet is filing-centered and segment-oriented. The 2025 Form 10-K identifies major operating groups including insurance, BNSF, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, manufacturing, service and retailing, and McLane, while SEC company facts provide fiscal 2025 revenue, net income, operating cash flow, and equity figures.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K names the main operating segment members, including Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Group, Burlington Northern Santa Fe Corporation, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, Manufacturing, Service and Retailing, and McLane.

The filing breaks out insurance operations by business type, including GEICO, reinsurance, primary insurance, auto liability, physical damage, workers' compensation, medical professional liability, property, and life and health insurance.
SEC company facts include FY2025 revenue of $254.906 billion, net income of $96.223 billion, operating cash flow of $49.196 billion, and stockholders' equity of $649.368 billion.

The proxy describes Berkshire's decentralized operating model, stating that operating businesses are managed with minimal parent involvement in day-to-day activities and human-capital decisions are left to each business.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Berkshire combines owner-oriented board criteria with concentrated voting power and decentralized operating oversight. For the 2026 annual meeting, 13 current directors were nominated and eight were identified as independent, while governance also has to oversee material subsidiary-level risks such as PacifiCorp wildfire liabilities.
points: For the 2026 annual meeting, Berkshire nominated 13 current directors, and the Governance Committee identified eight as independent, including Stephen B. Burke, Kenneth I. Chenault, and Susan L. Decker.

The board's governance approach reflects Berkshire's decentralized model, with workforce and human-capital decisions placed within each operating business's oversight and discretion rather than centralized parent control.

Berkshire does not provide directors and officers liability insurance to its directors, and non-employee director compensation is limited to meeting fees and Audit Committee service fees stated in the proxy.

A named operating risk is PacifiCorp wildfire liability: cumulative estimated probable losses were about $2.85 billion before taxes and expected recoveries, with about $1.2 billion unpaid at year-end 2025.

types.summary: Berkshire's balance sheet gives it unusual financial capacity across insurance, rail, energy, and operating subsidiaries. At December 31, 2025, the Parent Company held $14.627 billion of cash and $112.811 billion of U.S. Treasury Bills, while Berkshire reported $649.368 billion of stockholders' equity and $49.196 billion of FY2025 operating cash flow.

Insurance remains central enough that Berkshire's SEC industry classification is Fire, Marine & Casualty Insurance, and its operations include GEICO, reinsurance, primary insurance, auto liability, workers' compensation, property, and life and health lines. Ajit Jain's 39 years managing Berkshire reinsurance operations and pricing large, complex risks supports a company-specific capability in risk selection.

Berkshire is not dependent on a single product line or customer category in the packet. Its 2025 Form 10-K identifies operating groups across Berkshire Hathaway Insurance Group, BNSF, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, Manufacturing Businesses, Service and Retailing Businesses, and McLane, supporting FY2025 revenue of $254.906 billion.

Berkshire's Board describes the company as unusually decentralized, with minimal parent involvement in day-to-day activities and workforce decisions placed within each business's discretion. That model is specific to a holding company with large subsidiaries such as BNSF, Berkshire Hathaway Energy, McLane, insurance operations, manufacturing, service, and retailing businesses.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Citigroup is led by Jane Fraser as both Chair of the Board and Chief Executive Officer, with Mark A. L. Mason as Chief Financial Officer and Brent J. McIntosh as Chief Legal Officer and Corporate Secretary. The leadership team is managing a large national commercial bank while also executing a multiyear transformation tied to risk, controls, data quality, and internal control remediation.
points: Jane Fraser holds the combined Chair and CEO role, while John Dugan serves as Lead Independent Director, giving the board a named independent leadership counterweight.
Management is overseeing five reported operating segments: Services, Markets, Banking, Wealth, and US Personal Banking, which makes execution more complex than a single-line banking model.
The company says it has embarked on a multiyear transformation aimed at strengthening risk and controls while improving value to customers, clients, and shareholders.
Leadership still carries remediation burden from 2020 FRB and OCC consent orders and 2024 civil money penalty consent orders tied to remediation shortcomings.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Citigroup returned over $17.5 billion to stockholders in 2025, split between $13.250 billion of common stock repurchases and $4.340 billion of common dividends. Capital returns were made while the company ended 2025 with a 13.2% CET1 capital ratio, roughly 160 basis points above its minimum regulatory requirement.
points: The 2025 capital return mix leaned heavily toward buybacks, with $13.250 billion of common stock repurchases compared with $4.340 billion of common dividends.
Citigroup ended 2025 with $208.598 billion of stockholders' equity and long-term debt of $287.3 billion, underscoring the balance-sheet scale and leverage of the bank.
The company reported FY2025 net income of $9.228 billion on revenues of $78.066 billion, giving management room for returns but not eliminating execution risk.
Portfolio simplification continued through non-core exits outside the United States, including Banamex progress, an agreement to sell Poland consumer operations, and the AO Citibank Russia sale.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Citigroup's 2026 proxy gives investors several direct alignment markers: a 2025 capital return above $17.5 billion, an advisory vote on executive compensation, and a proposal to approve additional shares for the 2019 Stock Incentive Plan. The company also reports 2025 RoTCE of 8.8% excluding notable items, a useful profitability measure for common shareholders.
points: Common shareholders received both dividends and buybacks in 2025, with repurchases representing the larger portion of the more than $17.5 billion total return.
The proxy includes an advisory vote to approve 2025 executive compensation, giving stockholders a formal annual channel to express views on pay decisions.
The Annual Meeting materials ask shareholders to approve additional shares for the Citigroup 2019 Stock Incentive Plan, tying part of compensation governance to equity authorization.
Citigroup's 2025 RoTCE was 8.8% excluding notable items, while reported results also included Russia held-for-sale accounting impacts and a Mexico goodwill impairment charge.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Citigroup provides investors with formal SEC reporting that breaks the company into Services, Markets, Banking, Wealth, and US Personal Banking. Its 10-K glossary also defines specific business activities, including Treasury and Trade Solutions, Branded Cards, Retail Services, commercial cards, International region clusters, and Wealth net new investment asset flows.
points: The 10-K lists operating segments as Services, Markets, Banking, Wealth, and US Personal Banking, giving investors a structured view of the company's major business lines.
Citigroup's glossary defines Treasury and Trade Solutions as cash management and trade-related services, and commercial cards as payment services for corporate and public sector clients.
The company discloses Retail Services relationships by name, including The Home Depot, Best Buy, and Macy's, rather than describing the U.S. cards business only generically.
The 2026 proxy provides specific capital and profitability disclosures, including 2025 capital returns, a 13.2% CET1 ratio, and 8.8% RoTCE excluding notable items.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Citigroup's governance structure includes 13 directors up for election, Jane Fraser as combined Chair and CEO, and John Dugan as Lead Independent Director. The key governance issue for investors is oversight of risk and controls, because the company remains tied to 2020 FRB and OCC consent orders and 2024 civil money penalty consent orders addressing remediation shortcomings.
points: Stockholders are being asked to elect 13 directors at the 2026 Annual Meeting, giving investors a direct vote on the full board slate described in the materials.
Jane Fraser combines the Chair and CEO roles, while John Dugan is identified as Lead Independent Director, a relevant structure for board oversight assessment.
The board and management must oversee remediation tied to enterprise-wide risk management, compliance, data quality management related to governance, and internal controls.
Governance materials include both an advisory vote on 2025 executive compensation and a request for more shares under the 2019 Stock Incentive Plan.

types.summary: Citigroup has significant scale, with FY2025 revenues of $78.066 billion, net income of $9.228 billion, and stockholders' equity of $208.598 billion. The bank operates across Services, Markets, Banking, Wealth, and US Personal Banking, which gives it multiple revenue sources but also adds complexity and regulatory oversight demands.

Citigroup's International region spans six disclosed clusters: United Kingdom; Japan, Asia North and Australia; LATAM; Asia South; Europe; and Middle East, Africa and Russia. That footprint supports a broad banking network for multinational activity, although the company is also simplifying through non-core exits such as Banamex progress, Poland consumer sale agreement, and Russia sale completion.

types.type: Treasury, trade, and commercial card services
types.summary: Citigroup's Treasury and Trade Solutions business provides cash management and trade-related services, while commercial cards support procurement, corporate travel and entertainment, expense management, and business-to-business payments for corporate and public sector clients. These services can be embedded in client workflows, but the packet does not provide retention rates or market-share data.

Citigroup's U.S. Retail Services cards business includes co-brand and private label relationships with named retailers including The Home Depot, Best Buy, and Macy's. Its Branded Cards business also includes proprietary cards, co-branded cards, and personal installment loans, giving the company several consumer credit channels without proving a durable pricing advantage from the packet alone.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Caterpillar completed a clearly disclosed leadership transition in 2026, with Joseph E. Creed serving as Chairman and CEO after becoming chairman on April 1, 2026. The transition followed Jim Umpleby's retirement as Executive Chairman after more than four decades at Caterpillar, including eight years as Chairman and CEO.
points: Joseph E. Creed signed the 2025 Form 10-K as Chief Executive Officer and Director, and the 2026 proxy identifies him as Chairman and CEO.
Jim Umpleby retired as Executive Chairman on April 1, 2026, after more than four decades with Caterpillar and eight years as Chairman and CEO.
The 2025 Form 10-K identifies Andrew R.J. Bonfield as Chief Financial Officer and William E. Schaupp as Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Caterpillar produced substantial cash in 2025 and used it across reinvestment, shareholder returns, and acquisition activity. The company reported $12.885 billion of operating cash flow, $9.5 billion of MP&E free cash flow, $2.108 billion of R&D expense, and a planned approximately $790 million RPMGlobal acquisition.
points: Caterpillar returned $7.9 billion to shareholders in 2025 through share repurchases and dividends, compared with reported MP&E free cash flow of $9.5 billion.
The company reported $2.108 billion of research and development expense in FY2025, supporting product and technology investment across its machinery and energy businesses.

Caterpillar's planned acquisition of RPMGlobal, approved by the Federal Court of Australia, adds mining software capabilities for an expected purchase price of approximately $790 million excluding cash acquired.
At FY2025, Caterpillar reported $6.889 billion of cash and equivalents and $27.351 billion of long-term debt, so capital allocation includes meaningful balance sheet management.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Caterpillar's shareholder alignment is supported by large direct capital returns and governance policies tied to ownership and compensation oversight. The 2026 proxy reports director and executive officer stock ownership guidelines, anti-hedging and pledging policies, a clawback policy, and no equity compensation plans not approved by shareholders.
points: The company deployed $7.9 billion to shareholders through repurchases and dividends in 2025, a concrete return of cash against $9.5 billion of MP&E free cash flow.
Caterpillar lists director and executive officer stock ownership guidelines, anti-hedging and pledging policies, and a clawback policy among its 2026 proxy governance highlights.
As of December 31, 2025, all listed equity compensation plans were shareholder-approved, with no equity compensation plans not approved by shareholders disclosed.
The 2026 proxy reported 2025 profit per share of $18.81 versus $22.05 in 2024, giving shareholders a clear year-over-year profitability comparison.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Caterpillar provides investor-useful disclosure through SEC filings, XBRL segment tags, proxy performance metrics, and specific operating measures such as backlog, margins, free cash flow, and restructuring costs. Its 2025 Form 10-K also separates machinery, power, energy, and financial product reporting categories, including finance receivable types and geographic locations.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K XBRL tags identify operating categories including Construction Industries, Resource Industries, Power & Energy, Machinery/Power/Energy, and Financial Products.
The 2026 proxy reported 2025 sales and revenues of $67.6 billion, backlog of $51 billion, operating profit of $11.2 billion, and adjusted operating profit of $11.6 billion.
Caterpillar discloses Financial Products receivable categories including wholesale receivables, wholesale loans, wholesale leases, retail loans, and retail leases.
The 2025 Form 10-K states all restructuring costs in 2025, 2024, and 2023 were excluded from segment profit, clarifying how segment profitability is presented.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Caterpillar's governance framework includes a majority-independent board, annual elections, majority voting, proxy access, a lead independent director, and shareholder ability to call special meetings. The company also discloses specific compliance matters, including Iran trademark maintenance under an OFAC license with no revenues or profits from that activity.
points: The 2026 proxy lists 10 director nominees, including 9 independent directors, with average nominee tenure of 5.5 years and average director age of 65.
Caterpillar's 2026 proxy reports 50% women nominees and 30% ethnic or racial diversity among director nominees.
Governance highlights include annual director elections, majority voting in director elections, proxy access, no poison pill, and shareholder ability to call special meetings.
Caterpillar disclosed Iran trademark-maintenance activity under an OFAC specific license, approximately $608 paid for the period ended December 31, 2024, and no revenues or profits from the activity.

types.summary: Caterpillar's scale is evident in FY2025 revenue of $67.060 billion and proxy-reported 2025 sales and revenues of $67.6 billion, the highest full-year sales and revenues in company history. Its disclosed operating categories span Construction Industries, Resource Industries, Power & Energy, Machinery/Power/Energy, and Financial Products, giving the business multiple end-market exposures.

Caterpillar generated FY2025 operating cash flow of $12.885 billion and proxy-reported MP&E free cash flow of $9.5 billion. That cash base supported $2.108 billion of R&D expense, $7.9 billion of shareholder returns, and an enterprise cash balance of $10.0 billion at the end of 2025.

types.type: Financial Products support for equipment sales
types.summary: Caterpillar's Financial Products business adds a financing layer to its machinery model through wholesale receivables, wholesale loans, wholesale leases, retail loans, and retail leases. The packet does not quantify financing market share or customer retention, but these categories show the company can support both dealer and end-user equipment financing needs.

Caterpillar is expanding its mining technology position through the approved acquisition of RPMGlobal Holdings Limited for an expected approximately $790 million excluding cash acquired. The 2025 Form 10-K describes RPMGlobal as a provider of mining software solutions with data-driven tools across every stage of the mining lifecycle.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Costco is led by Ron M. Vachris as Chief Executive Officer, President and Director, with Gary Millerchip as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer. The leadership team is running a large, operationally complex retailer with 914 warehouses, e-commerce sites in eight markets, and $275.235 billion of fiscal 2025 total revenue.
points: Ron M. Vachris signed the 2025 Form 10-K as Chief Executive Officer, President and Director, placing one named executive over both corporate leadership and board-level accountability.

Gary Millerchip signed as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer, while Tiffany M. Barbre signed as Senior Vice President and Corporate Controller, anchoring financial reporting responsibilities.

Costco employed 341,000 people worldwide at fiscal 2025 year-end, with about 95% in membership warehouses and distribution channels, making execution quality heavily dependent on store and logistics operations.
The company reported approximately 94% retention in the U.S. and Canada for employees with at least one year of service, a useful operating marker for a labor-intensive warehouse model.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Capital allocation is directed toward warehouse growth, distribution capacity, and international expansion rather than a complicated mix of unrelated businesses. Costco added property and equipment of $5.498 billion in fiscal 2025 and expanded from 861 warehouses in 2023 to 914 warehouses at August 31, 2025.
points: Fiscal 2025 additions to property and equipment totaled $5.498 billion, including $4.215 billion in the United States, $580 million in Canada, and $703 million in Other International.
The warehouse count rose to 914 at August 31, 2025 from 890 a year earlier and 861 two years earlier, showing continued reinvestment in the core format.

Capital spending supports a model built around volume purchasing, efficient distribution, no-frills facilities, and rapid inventory turnover, which Costco says enables profitability at lower gross margins than most retailers.
Management also allocates space and investment to ancillary businesses, including 747 gas stations; gasoline represented approximately 10% of fiscal 2025 total net sales.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The packet supports a practical alignment case: executive pay is meaningfully tied to performance-based restricted stock units, and the business model depends on renewing paid members through low prices. Costco's 81.0 million paid members and high renewal rates make member value, traffic, and long-term sales volume central to shareholder outcomes.
points: The proxy states that restricted stock units are the largest component of executive compensation if fully earned through achievement of performance targets, linking a major pay element to performance conditions.
Costco's model depends on offering low prices on a limited selection of branded and private-label goods, aiming for high sales volumes and rapid inventory turnover rather than high per-item margins.
At fiscal 2025 year-end, Costco had 81.0 million paid members and 145.2 million total cardholders, making membership value a direct operating driver for revenue durability.
Executive members totaled 38.7 million and accounted for approximately 73.6% of worldwide net sales in 2025, concentrating sales around members who pay for the higher-tier relationship.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Costco provides unusually concrete operating disclosure for a retailer in the provided filings, including member counts, renewal rates, warehouse counts, category sales, segment revenue, and segment operating income. The company also separates e-commerce and digitally enabled sales, gasoline exposure, employee data, and named legal matters in its 2025 Form 10-K.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K discloses total paid members, Gold Star members, Business members, total cardholders, Executive members, and renewal rates for the U.S. and Canada and worldwide.
Costco reports segment results for the United States, Canada, and Other International, including fiscal 2025 total revenue and operating income for each segment.
The company discloses merchandise category net sales for Foods and Sundries, Non-Foods, Fresh Foods, and Warehouse Ancillary and Other Businesses, giving investors a view of sales mix.
Costco separately reports e-commerce net sales at approximately 7% of fiscal 2025 total net sales and digitally enabled sales at approximately 10% of total net sales.

The 2025 Form 10-K identifies pending matters including wage-and-hour and PAGA actions, opioid litigation, privacy-law suits, a PFAS false-advertising case, a False Claims Act investigation, and an EPA FIFRA matter.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance disclosure includes a named chairman, board nominees, an independent Compensation Committee, and a performance-based equity-heavy executive pay structure. The governance picture is balanced by operating and legal risks disclosed in the 10-K, including intense retail competition and multiple pending legal and regulatory matters.
points: Hamilton E. James signed the 2025 Form 10-K as Chairman of the Board, while Ron M. Vachris also serves as Chief Executive Officer, President and Director.
The proxy states that the Board had ten nominees for election at the 2026 annual meeting, giving investors a specific board slate to evaluate.
The Compensation Committee is composed of independent directors, and restricted stock units are the largest executive compensation component if fully earned through performance targets.

Costco identifies significant U.S. competitors including Walmart, Target, Kroger, Amazon, Sam's Club, and BJ's Wholesale Club, highlighting governance oversight needs in a highly competitive industry.

Disclosed legal and regulatory matters include California wage-and-hour and PAGA actions, opioid-related litigation, privacy-law suits tied to pixel trackers on Costco.com, and a False Claims Act investigation.

types.summary: Costco's paid membership base is a concrete source of recurring customer relationship value. At fiscal 2025 year-end, the company had 81.0 million paid members, 145.2 million total cardholders, and renewal rates of 92.3% in the U.S. and Canada and 89.8% worldwide, excluding affiliates of Business members.

Costco's $269.912 billion of fiscal 2025 net sales gives it large purchasing and distribution scale to support its low-price model. The company says volume purchasing, efficient distribution, reduced handling in no-frills self-service warehouses, and rapid inventory turnover allow it to operate profitably at significantly lower gross margins than most other retailers.

Costco's core warehouse assortment is intentionally narrow, with less than 4,000 active SKUs per warehouse, compared with 9,000 to 10,000 SKUs online. This limited selection supports purchasing concentration, simpler handling, and rapid inventory turnover across major categories such as Foods and Sundries, Non-Foods, Fresh Foods, and Warehouse Ancillary.

Costco's 914 warehouses across the United States and Puerto Rico, Canada, Mexico, Japan, the United Kingdom, Korea, Australia, Taiwan, China, Spain, France, Sweden, Iceland, and New Zealand create a broad physical network. Ancillary services deepen the warehouse trip, including gasoline, pharmacy, optical, food court, hearing aids, and tire installation.

Executive members are a concentrated driver of Costco's sales base. In fiscal 2025, Executive members represented 38.7 million paid members and accounted for approximately 73.6% of worldwide net sales, making the higher-tier membership group central to Costco's traffic, basket size, and renewal economics.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: CrowdStrike is led around a single consolidated operating segment, with the Chief Executive Officer identified as the chief operating decision maker. The leadership team delivered fiscal 2026 revenue growth of 22%, ending ARR growth of 24%, and completed a workforce reduction plan while continuing to invest heavily in research and development.
points: Management runs CrowdStrike as one operating and reportable segment, which fits a unified cloud-native cybersecurity platform across endpoints, cloud workloads, identity, and data.
Fiscal 2026 execution included $4.81 billion of revenue, $5.25 billion of ending ARR, and 97% gross retention in each quarter of the year.
CrowdStrike reduced roles by about 500 positions, or 5% of its global workforce, under a May 2025 strategic plan substantially completed by January 31, 2026.
The company still reported $780.319 million of fiscal 2026 research and development expense, showing continued product investment alongside restructuring actions.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Capital allocation is active and balanced between acquisitions, internal investment, cash generation, and share repurchases. CrowdStrike generated $1.24 billion of free cash flow in fiscal 2026, but also committed nearly $1 billion of net cash to two acquisitions shortly after year-end, making integration and return on capital important to watch.
points: CrowdStrike reported $1.61 billion of operating cash flow in fiscal 2026, equal to 34% of revenue, and $1.24 billion of free cash flow, a 26% margin.
The company had $3.375069 billion of cash and cash equivalents, $743.983 million of long-term debt, and $3.279494 billion of stockholders' equity for FY2026.
CrowdStrike bought 143,801 Class A shares for $50.6 million after January 31, 2026, at an average price of $351.97 per share.
The company closed SGNL.AI for $627.9 million net cash and Seraphic Algorithms for $327.4 million net cash, adding sizable acquisition execution risk.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Shareholder alignment is supported by cash generation, a repurchase authorization, and executive trading plans that are disclosed in SEC filings. The packet does not provide insider ownership percentages or detailed incentive metrics, so alignment should be judged mainly through disclosed capital returns, equity-related plans, and operating performance.
points: About $949.4 million remained available under CrowdStrike's share repurchase program as of March 4, 2026, after $50.6 million of recent repurchases.
George Kurtz adopted a Rule 10b5-1 trading arrangement covering up to 627,500 shares during the three months ended January 31, 2026.
Michael Sentonas modified a Rule 10b5-1 plan covering up to 100,000 shares, and Anurag Saha modified a plan covering up to 9,016 shares.
Fiscal 2026 net income was $72.181 million, while free cash flow was $1.24 billion, so investors should distinguish accounting earnings from cash generation.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: CrowdStrike provides investors with ARR, retention, free cash flow, operating cash flow, module adoption, Falcon Flex ARR, and product-category ARR metrics. It also states that the business is reported as a single consolidated segment, which simplifies reporting but limits visibility into profitability by product family.
points: The company disclosed total revenue of $4.81 billion, ending ARR of $5.25 billion, and 24% year-over-year ARR growth as of January 31, 2026.
CrowdStrike reported that gross retention was 97% in each quarter of fiscal 2026, giving investors a concrete recurring-revenue durability metric.
The company disclosed module adoption levels: 50% of customers used six or more modules, 34% used seven or more, and 24% used eight or more, excluding Falcon Go customers.
CrowdStrike reports as a single operating and reportable segment, so investors do not receive separate segment economics for endpoint, cloud, identity, data, LogScale, or SIEM offerings.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance disclosures include effective disclosure controls, effective internal control over financial reporting, and formal executive Rule 10b5-1 trading-plan reporting. The packet does not describe board composition, committee structure, or compensation scorecards, so governance cannot be graded as strong on the available facts alone.
points: Management, the CEO, and the CFO concluded disclosure controls and procedures were effective as of January 31, 2026.
Management concluded internal control over financial reporting was effective as of January 31, 2026, according to the fiscal 2026 10-K disclosure.
Named trading-plan disclosures covered George Kurtz, Michael Sentonas, and Anurag Saha, giving investors visibility into planned potential share sales.
Operating risks visible in the packet include acquisition integration demands, $44.8 million of strategic-plan charges, and a 5% workforce reduction completed during fiscal 2026.

types.summary: CrowdStrike's Falcon platform shows evidence of customer expansion across multiple security modules rather than single-product use. As of January 31, 2026, 50% of customers had adopted six or more modules, 34% had adopted seven or more, and 24% had adopted eight or more, excluding Falcon Go customers.

The business has a large recurring base, with ending ARR of $5.25 billion as of January 31, 2026, up 24% year over year. CrowdStrike also reported 97% gross retention in each quarter of fiscal 2026, indicating that existing subscription revenue was highly retained during the year.

CrowdStrike's platform spans enterprise endpoints, cloud workloads, identity, and data, giving it multiple areas to cross-sell within cybersecurity budgets. ARR from cloud security, next-gen identity protection, and LogScale next-gen SIEM together surpassed $1.9 billion as of January 31, 2026.

CrowdStrike has reached meaningful operating scale for a software security company, with fiscal 2026 revenue of $4.81 billion and operating cash flow of $1.61 billion. Free cash flow was $1.24 billion, a 26% margin, supporting continued R&D, acquisitions, and repurchases.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Cisco is led by Charles H. Robbins as Chair and Chief Executive Officer, with Mark Patterson as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer and M. Victoria Wong as Senior Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer. Management's stated direction is to "securely connect everything," adding AI across networking, security, collaboration, and observability while addressing infrastructure, cyber threat, and AI-data needs.
points: Charles H. Robbins signed the fiscal 2025 Form 10-K as Chair and CEO, while Mark Patterson and M. Victoria Wong signed as the senior finance and accounting executives.
Cisco's chief executive officer is also the chief operating decision maker, and the company measures geographic segment performance using segment revenue and segment gross margin.
Management frames the company around three customer priorities: building modern infrastructure, protecting against cyber threats, and harnessing AI and data across IT environments.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Cisco returned substantial cash while still funding product development and maintaining a large balance sheet. In fiscal 2025, the company returned $12.4 billion to stockholders through dividends and repurchases, equal to 94% of free cash flow, while reporting $7.551 billion of R&D expense and $19.621 billion of long-term debt.
points: The Chair and CEO stated that Cisco returned $12.4 billion to stockholders in fiscal 2025 through dividends and share repurchases, representing 94% of free cash flow.
Cisco funded $7.551 billion of R&D expense in FY2025, supporting product portfolios across networking, security, collaboration, and observability.
The balance sheet included $7.508 billion of cash and equivalents, $19.621 billion of long-term debt, and $39.773 billion of stockholders' equity for FY2025.
A supplier dispute settlement released about $563 million from escrow but forfeited about $450 million of supplier-held prepayments and caused a $355 million product cost-of-sales charge.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Cisco's shareholder alignment is supported by explicit capital returns, compensation guardrails, and documented investor engagement. The proxy says most annual total direct compensation is performance-based, incentives are capped, executives face ownership and holding requirements, and the company engaged stockholders representing about 61% of shares outstanding in fiscal 2025.
points: Executive compensation practices include performance-based majority annual total direct compensation, caps on incentive compensation, stock ownership guidelines, holding requirements, and a clawback policy.
Cisco's proxy lists shareholder-friendly limits including no single-trigger vesting of equity awards, no golden parachute tax gross-ups, and no supplemental executive retirement plan.
Cisco engaged with stockholders representing approximately 61% of shares outstanding at fiscal year-end 2025, including holders representing 30% of its top 30 stockholders.
Capital returns were material in fiscal 2025, with $12.4 billion returned through dividends and repurchases, equal to 94% of free cash flow.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Cisco's disclosures give investors useful operating detail by product, service, geography, customer concentration, and segment gross margin. The 10-K reports product and services revenue, revenue by Networking, Security, Collaboration, and Observability, and Americas, EMEA, and APJC revenue and gross margin, while also identifying the CEO as the chief operating decision maker.
points: Cisco disclosed fiscal 2025 product revenue of $41.608 billion and services revenue of $15.046 billion, separating hardware-oriented product sales from associated service revenue.
The company reported product category revenue of $28.304 billion from Networking, $8.094 billion from Security, $4.154 billion from Collaboration, and $1.055 billion from Observability.
Segment reporting is geographic, with fiscal 2025 Americas, EMEA, and APJC revenue and gross margin disclosed separately for investor analysis.
Cisco disclosed that no single customer represented 10% or more of revenue in fiscal 2025, 2024, or 2023, helping investors assess customer concentration risk.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Cisco combines a Chair-CEO structure with a named Lead Independent Director, Michael D. Capellas, and the proxy lists several shareholder-rights provisions. Governance disclosures include annual director elections, majority voting in uncontested elections, a majority-independent board, written-consent rights, a 10% special-meeting threshold, no poison pill, and detailed executive compensation limits.
points: The proxy identifies Charles H. Robbins as Board Chair and CEO and Michael D. Capellas as Lead Independent Director, providing a named independent board leadership role.
Cisco lists annual director elections, majority voting for uncontested director elections, a majority-independent board, stockholder written-consent rights, and no poison pill.
Shareholders have the right to call a special meeting at a 10% ownership threshold, according to Cisco's proxy governance practices.
Risk-related disclosures include $2.337 billion of gross unrecognized tax benefits and the supplier settlement that produced a $355 million fiscal fourth-quarter cost-of-sales charge.

types.type: Scale across networking and adjacent IT categories
types.summary: Cisco generated $56.654 billion of fiscal 2025 revenue, including $28.304 billion from Networking, $8.094 billion from Security, $4.154 billion from Collaboration, and $1.055 billion from Observability. That breadth gives Cisco a large operating base across the communications and IT industry without relying on a single disclosed product category alone.

Cisco designs and sells IP-based networking and related communications and IT products, and it also provides services associated with those products and their use. In fiscal 2025, the business produced $41.608 billion of product revenue and $15.046 billion of services revenue, showing that customer relationships extend beyond initial product sales.

Cisco is managed globally through the Americas, EMEA, and APJC, with fiscal 2025 revenue of $33.656 billion, $14.824 billion, and $8.174 billion in those segments, respectively. No single customer accounted for 10% or more of revenue in fiscal 2025, 2024, or 2023, reducing dependence on any one buyer.

types.type: R&D capacity and AI product direction
types.summary: Cisco reported $7.551 billion of R&D expense for FY2025 and says it is incorporating artificial intelligence into networking, security, collaboration, and observability. The stated strategy is to integrate products more tightly together while addressing modern infrastructure, cyber threats, and AI-and-data priorities for customers.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Chevron is led by Michael K. Wirth as Chairman and CEO, with Eimear P. Bonner as CFO and Alana K. Knowles as Controller signing the 2025 10-K. The leadership team is managing a large integrated energy company focused on growing oil and gas, lowering operational carbon intensity, and building new energies businesses.
points: Wirth's 2025 shareholder letter highlighted record production of 3.7 million barrels of oil equivalent per day, a 12% increase from the prior year.
Chevron's operating agenda is asset-specific: Permian production surpassed one million barrels of oil equivalent per day, while Denver-Julesberg and Bakken reached roughly 600,000 combined.
Management completed the Hess Corp. acquisition, adding a 30% ownership interest in the Guyana Stabroek Block and increasing Gulf of America production to over 300,000 barrels per day.
Tengizchevroil's Future Growth Project started up and reached design capacity of more than one million gross barrels of oil equivalent per day in less than 30 days.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Chevron's capital allocation is explicitly built around rewarding stockholders, investing efficiently, maintaining the balance sheet, and repurchasing shares regularly. In 2025, the company generated $16.592 billion of free cash flow and returned $27.1 billion to stockholders through dividends, Chevron repurchases, and Hess share purchases before closing.
points: Chevron reconciled 2025 free cash flow as $33.939 billion of operating cash flow less $17.347 billion of capital expenditures, leaving $16.592 billion.
The company returned $12.8 billion through dividends and $12.1 billion through Chevron share repurchases, plus $2.2 billion of Hess shares before the merger closed.
Balance sheet metrics remained moderate for a commodity business, with 2025 debt-to-CFFO of 1.2x, net debt-to-CFFO of 1.0x, and a 15.6% net debt ratio.
Chevron reported $40.758 billion of total debt and $6.293 billion of cash and equivalents at year-end 2025, producing $34.461 billion of net debt.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Chevron's stated financial priorities begin with rewarding stockholders first by reliably growing the dividend, followed by efficient capital investment, balance sheet strength, and regular buybacks. The 2025 capital return program was large relative to free cash flow, showing management's willingness to distribute cash while still funding major projects and acquisitions.
points: The 2025 proxy states Chevron's objective is higher returns, lower carbon, and superior stockholder value in any business environment, tying strategy directly to owner outcomes.
Stockholder returns were not abstract: the company disclosed $27.1 billion returned in 2025, including cash dividends, Chevron buybacks, and Hess share purchases.
Executive pay remains subject to an advisory vote, and the 10-K exhibit index lists incentive plans, long-term incentive award forms, deferred compensation plans, and a Dodd-Frank clawback policy.
Shareholder alignment is balanced by capital intensity: Chevron spent $17.347 billion on capital expenditures in 2025 while pursuing production growth and Hess integration.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Chevron provides investors with detailed SEC-based reporting across segments, capital returns, leverage, free cash flow, reserves, and operating production milestones. The company's disclosures are especially useful because they connect financial outcomes with specific assets such as the Permian, Denver-Julesberg, Bakken, Gulf of America, Tengizchevroil, and Guyana Stabroek.
points: The 2025 10-K XBRL segment disclosures separate U.S. and non-U.S. Upstream and Downstream members for the 2023, 2024, and 2025 periods.
Chevron's filings identify key affiliates by segment, including Tengizchevroil, Caspian Pipeline Consortium, and Angola LNG in Upstream, and Chevron Phillips Chemical and GS Caltex in Downstream.
The proxy appendix gives a clear free cash flow bridge from operating cash flow to capital expenditures and also reports debt, net debt, and leverage ratios.

The CEO letter reports concrete operating milestones, including record companywide production, Permian volumes, Denver-Julesberg and Bakken output, Gulf of America production, and Tengizchevroil ramp-up.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Chevron has identifiable governance structure and board oversight, including Dr. Wanda M. Austin as Lead Director and Mary A. Francis as Corporate Secretary and Chief Governance Officer. The Board reviews strategy across scenarios, policy shifts, and technology disruptions, but investors should also weigh climate, coastal erosion, Indigenous rights, and human rights-related governance scrutiny disclosed in filings.
points: The Lead Director letter says the Board regularly reviews company strategy across multiple scenarios, shifting policy environments, and potential technology disruptions.
The Board maintains active oversight of capital allocation and significant portfolio decisions, which is important given Chevron's large capital program and Hess acquisition.

Chevron's 2026 annual meeting includes an advisory vote on named executive officer compensation, and filed plans include incentive, long-term incentive, deferred compensation, restoration, insider trading, and clawback policies.
The Board recommended against proposals to adopt an independent chair, report on Indigenous Peoples' Rights, and commission a third-party report on human rights processes.
The 2025 10-K includes pending litigation tags for Climate Change and Coastal Erosion, with settled Coastal Erosion litigation tags in April 2025.

types.type: Integrated oil and gas asset scale
types.summary: Chevron operates at a scale that few energy companies can match, with FY2025 revenue of $196.913 billion and record production of 3.7 million barrels of oil equivalent per day. Its segment structure spans U.S. and non-U.S. Upstream and Downstream operations, supported by affiliates such as Tengizchevroil, Angola LNG, Chevron Phillips Chemical, and GS Caltex.

Chevron's proved reserve base supports long-duration production, with 1.348 billion barrels of natural gas liquids and 29.245 trillion cubic feet of natural gas in consolidated and affiliated net proved reserves at December 31, 2025. The reserve moat is real but commodity-sensitive, as standardized measure net cash flows declined to $111.822 billion from higher levels in 2024 and 2023.

types.type: Portfolio depth in major basins and projects
types.summary: Chevron's production base is spread across several large assets rather than one project: Permian production surpassed one million barrels of oil equivalent per day, Denver-Julesberg and Bakken reached roughly 600,000 combined, and Gulf of America exceeded 300,000 in 2025. The Hess acquisition also added a 30% ownership interest in the Guyana Stabroek Block.

types.type: Balance sheet capacity for commodity cycles
types.summary: Chevron's balance sheet gives it flexibility in a cyclical industry, with 2025 debt-to-CFFO of 1.2x, net debt-to-CFFO of 1.0x, and a 15.6% net debt ratio. That financial position supports dividends, buybacks, acquisitions, and major capital projects, but it is not a standalone moat without continued operating cash flow from commodity-linked assets.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Dell is led by founder Michael S. Dell, who signed the 2026 Form 10-K as Chairman, Chief Executive Officer, and principal executive officer. The finance organization is named in the filing as well, with David Kennedy as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer and Richard Troy Sharp as Chief Accounting Officer.
points: Michael S. Dell remains the central executive figure, signing the 2026 Form 10-K as Chairman, Chief Executive Officer, and principal executive officer.
David Kennedy signed the 2026 Form 10-K as Executive Vice President, Chief Financial Officer, and principal financial officer, giving investors a named accountable finance leader.
Richard Troy Sharp signed as Senior Vice President, Corporate Finance and Chief Accounting Officer, making the principal accounting officer identifiable in Dell's annual filing.
Michael Dell's 2026 proxy letter positioned Dell's AI opportunity across at-scale neoclouds, enterprise workloads, and edge computing through the PC.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Dell returned substantial capital in fiscal 2026 while still carrying a leveraged balance sheet. The proxy reports $7.5 billion returned through repurchases and dividends, while SEC company facts show $19.363 billion of long-term debt, negative $1.482 billion of stockholders' equity, and $3.633 billion of cash.
points: Dell's 2026 proxy reports $7.5 billion of shareholder return in fiscal 2026 through a combination of share repurchases and dividends.
SEC company facts report FY2026 operating cash flow of $8.676 billion, giving Dell internally generated cash to support investment, debt service, and shareholder returns.
Dell also reported FY2026 research and development expense of $2.801 billion, showing capital allocation to product and technology investment alongside shareholder distributions.
The balance sheet remains a key constraint: SEC company facts report $19.363 billion of long-term debt and negative $1.482 billion of stockholders' equity.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Dell has explicit shareholder-return activity and equity-based compensation tools, but the ownership and voting structure is complex. The record includes stockholder agreements involving Michael S. Dell, the Susan Lieberman Dell Separate Property Trust, Silver Lake entities, and management stockholders, plus multiple director-election classes.
points: Fiscal 2026 shareholder return was meaningful in absolute dollars, with the proxy reporting $7.5 billion returned through share repurchases and dividends.
Dell's 2023 Stock Incentive Plan includes time-based restricted stock units, performance-based restricted stock units, deferred stock units, and performance-based stock option agreements.
The Form 10-K exhibit list includes an Incentive-Based Compensation Recovery Policy effective September 28, 2023, adding a stated clawback mechanism to incentive pay.
Alignment is not simple for outside holders because the filings reference stockholder agreements involving Michael S. Dell, Silver Lake entities, management stockholders, and related trusts.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Dell gives investors multiple formal disclosure channels, including the 2026 Form 10-K, SEC company facts, XBRL segment references, and the 2026 proxy statement. The packet supports specific reporting around Infrastructure Solutions Group, Client Solutions, All Other Segments, product and service categories, executive roles, capital returns, and compensation matters.
points: Dell's 2026 Form 10-K XBRL references operating segments including Infrastructure Solutions Group, Client Solutions, and All Other Segments.
The 2026 Form 10-K XBRL includes product and service revenue and cost category members, indicating reporting across product and service categories.
The 2026 proxy provides operating highlights including fiscal 2026 net revenue of $113.5 billion, operating income of $8.1 billion, and non-GAAP operating income of $10.0 billion.
SEC company facts separately report FY2026 revenue of $88.425 billion, net income of $3.388 billion, operating cash flow of $8.676 billion, and R&D expense of $2.801 billion.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Dell's governance disclosures identify directors, voting mechanics, auditor ratification, compensation votes, and major stockholder agreements. The structure deserves investor attention because seven Group I directors are elected by Class A, Class B, and Class C holders voting together, while the Group IV director is elected by Class C holders separately.
points: The 2026 proxy lists Group I director nominees Michael S. Dell, David W. Dorman, Egon Durban, David Grain, William D. Green, Ellen J. Kullman, and Steven M. Mollenkopf.
Lynn Vojvodich Radakovich is listed as the Group IV director nominee, elected by holders of Class C common stock voting separately as a series.
The board recommended redomesticating Dell Technologies Inc. from Delaware to Texas by conversion at the 2026 annual meeting.
The board recommended ratification of PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP as independent registered public accounting firm for the fiscal year ending January 29, 2027.
The proxy includes a non-binding advisory vote on named executive officer compensation, and the board recommended voting for the proposal.
The Form 10-K XBRL references one-customer concentration risk and the three largest contract manufacturers, which are concrete operating risks for board oversight.

types.type: Scale across computing infrastructure and PCs
types.summary: Dell has large operating scale, with the 2026 proxy reporting fiscal 2026 net revenue of $113.5 billion and SEC company facts reporting FY2026 operating cash flow of $8.676 billion. Its reported segments include Infrastructure Solutions Group and Client Solutions, tying the company to both enterprise infrastructure and client devices rather than a single product line.

Dell's 2026 Form 10-K XBRL includes product and service revenue and cost category members, and references operating segments including Infrastructure Solutions Group, Client Solutions, and All Other Segments. That breadth can help Dell participate in infrastructure, client devices, and related service categories, but the packet does not quantify segment margins or share positions.

Michael S. Dell's 2026 proxy letter said the company is positioned to extend AI from the largest at-scale neoclouds into enterprise workloads and out to the edge with the PC. This is a company-specific strategic position, but the packet does not provide AI revenue, backlog, customer counts, or profitability by AI offering.

SEC company facts report FY2026 research and development expense of $2.801 billion, giving Dell a measurable internal investment base for product and technology work. The moat evidence is moderate because the packet does not identify patents, proprietary platforms, win rates, or product-level returns from that spending.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Disney is led by Robert A. Iger as Chief Executive Officer, with James P. Gorman serving as Chairman of the Board. Management is operating a large three-part business-Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences-while also managing a pending CEO succession process that the Board currently expects to conclude with an announcement in early 2026.
points: The proxy identifies Disney's operating businesses as Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences, giving management responsibility across content, sports distribution, streaming, parks, cruise ships, and related consumer-facing operations.
Robert A. Iger remains Chief Executive Officer while the Board's Succession Planning Committee leads management succession oversight, making leadership continuity a key near-term investor issue.
Management reported concrete execution milestones in fiscal 2025, including Entertainment DTC operating income of $1.3 billion and ESPN's first full direct-to-consumer service launch.
Disney is also running a major Experiences expansion pipeline, including Magic Kingdom's largest-ever expansion, five additional cruise ships beyond fiscal 2026, and a planned Abu Dhabi theme park.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Disney generated FY2025 operating cash flow of $9.866 billion and ended the year with $14.182 billion of cash against $38.970 billion of long-term debt. Capital allocation is balanced between shareholder returns, debt and currency risk management, streaming integration, and a large Experiences investment pipeline that will require disciplined execution.
points: Management said Disney increased its dividend by 50% to $1.50 and increased its target share repurchase by 100% in fiscal 2026.
The balance sheet has scale, with $100.696 billion of stockholders' equity, but leverage remains material because long-term debt was $38.970 billion at FY2025.
Disney uses swaps and hedges to manage financing and currency exposure, including $9.3 billion of net foreign exchange cash flow hedges at September 27, 2025.
The Experiences pipeline is capital-intensive, with projects including major park expansion, five additional cruise ships beyond fiscal 2026, and a new planned Abu Dhabi theme park.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The packet shows explicit actions aimed at shareholder returns and incentive alignment, including a higher dividend, a larger repurchase target, and compensation changes tied to performance. Alignment is not founder-led in the packet; ownership is led by large institutions, with Vanguard and BlackRock disclosed as the two principal holders.
points: Disney disclosed The Vanguard Group as holder of 8.5% of shares and BlackRock, Inc. as holder of 6.9% of shares outstanding as of January 15, 2026.
The Compensation Committee made targeted fiscal 2025 enhancements intended to strengthen the link between executive compensation and performance and align incentives with shareholder interests.
The dividend increase to $1.50 and the doubled fiscal 2026 repurchase target give shareholders a direct capital return framework, supported by FY2025 operating cash flow.
Shareholder alignment must be weighed against large reinvestment needs, including theme park expansion, cruise ship growth, streaming integration, and sports direct-to-consumer distribution.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Disney's filings provide investors with useful segment and operating disclosures, including the Entertainment, Sports, and Experiences structure, fiscal 2025 total segment operating income, and Entertainment DTC profitability. The proxy and 10-K also disclose strategic updates, capital return targets, risk factors, hedging amounts, leases, and active litigation categories.
points: The proxy's non-GAAP reconciliation shows total segment operating income of $17.551 billion for fiscal 2025, alongside FY2025 revenue of $88.898 billion and net income of $2.354 billion.
Disney disclosed Entertainment DTC operating income of $1.3 billion and described the planned Disney+ and Hulu unified one-app experience.
Management reported sports distribution progress by stating ESPN launched its full direct-to-consumer service and enhanced ESPN app with its complete suite available directly to consumers.
The 10-K and proxy list specific risks, including content and talent competition, subscriber churn, regulatory developments, labor stoppages, weather and natural disasters, and content availability.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance is structured around a named chairman, a Board-led succession process, director nominees, and a Compensation Committee framework tied to performance. The main governance watch item is transition risk: the Board expects to announce the next CEO in early 2026 while Disney also faces legal, regulatory, labor, technology, and consumer-demand risks.
points: James P. Gorman is identified as Chairman of the Board, and the proxy identifies eleven director nominees for election at the 2026 annual meeting.
The Board's Succession Planning Committee leads management succession oversight, and the Board currently expects to announce Disney's next CEO in early 2026.
The Compensation Committee made fiscal 2025 enhancements to strengthen the link between executive compensation and performance and align incentives with shareholder interests.
Disney disclosed active litigation including securities, fiduciary duty, antitrust, and other business-related claims, with possible losses not reasonably estimable in certain cases.

types.summary: Disney's content engine showed unusually high box office output in the packet: management said six franchise films each generated more than $1 billion globally over the past two years. For calendar 2025 alone, Disney said three films exceeded $1 billion globally and total global box office receipts exceeded $6.5 billion.

Disney is turning its media assets into direct consumer products, with Entertainment DTC generating $1.3 billion of operating income in fiscal 2025. Management also said Disney is integrating Disney+ and Hulu into one app, while ESPN launched its full direct-to-consumer service and enhanced app for the first time.

Disney's Experiences moat is supported by hard-to-replicate physical assets and a large expansion pipeline. Management said it has more theme park expansion projects underway globally than ever before, including Magic Kingdom's largest-ever expansion, five additional cruise ships scheduled beyond fiscal 2026, and a new theme park planned for Abu Dhabi.

Disney operates at large scale, with FY2025 revenue of $88.898 billion, total segment operating income of $17.551 billion, and operating cash flow of $9.866 billion.
