That scale helps fund content, sports distribution, streaming integration, parks, cruises, dividends, and buybacks, though long-term debt of $38.970 billion limits the grade.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: General Electric now operates as GE Aerospace, led by Chairman and CEO H. Lawrence Culp, Jr., with Rahul Ghai as CFO and Robert Giglietti as chief accounting officer, controller, and treasurer. Management is running a focused aerospace business with two reportable segments, large installed bases, and a lean operating model tied to safety, quality, delivery, and cost.
points: GE Aerospace reports through Commercial Engines & Services and Defense & Propulsion Technologies, giving leadership a more focused operating scope than the former conglomerate structure.
FLIGHT DECK is described as GE Aerospace's proprietary lean operating model, prioritizing safety, quality, delivery, and cost in that order across the business.
The proxy says FLIGHT DECK helped increase material input from priority suppliers by more than 40% year over year and enabled 26% higher total engine deliveries in 2025.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Capital allocation in 2025 balanced investment, research, and shareholder returns. GE Aerospace invested $1 billion in U.S. manufacturing, funded roughly $3 billion of total R&D including customer and partner funding, and returned nearly $9 billion to shareholders while generating $7.694 billion of non-GAAP free cash flow.
points: The company funded roughly $3 billion of total R&D in 2025, including customer and partner funding, which fits an engine business where product durability and certification matter.
GE Aerospace returned nearly $9 billion to shareholders in 2025, a large distribution relative to reported non-GAAP free cash flow of $7.694 billion.
The balance sheet carried $19.342 billion of stockholders' equity and $20.469 billion of long-term debt at FY2025, so leverage remains an important capital allocation consideration.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The strongest shareholder-alignment evidence is economic: management returned nearly $9 billion to shareholders while still investing in manufacturing and R&D. The packet does not provide executive ownership or compensation-plan details, so alignment is best judged from capital returns, cash generation, and focus on profitable aerospace segments.
points: GE Aerospace reported 2025 GAAP profit of $10.000 billion, GAAP profit margin of 21.8%, cash from operating activities of $8.543 billion, and non-GAAP free cash flow of $7.694 billion.
Commercial Engines & Services produced $8.861 billion of 2025 segment profit, making the approximately 50,000-engine commercial installed base central to shareholder value.
The provided facts do not describe executive stock ownership or incentive-compensation metrics, limiting a deeper assessment of whether pay is directly tied to per-share outcomes.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: GE Aerospace gives investors useful operating detail through SEC filings and the proxy, including segment revenue, equipment versus services revenue, segment profit, orders, backlog, and remaining performance obligations. The disclosures are especially helpful because they separate equipment and services economics and provide timing expectations for equipment-related and services-related RPO.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K reports revenue by segment and by equipment versus services, including $33.314 billion for Commercial Engines & Services and $10.554 billion for Defense & Propulsion Technologies.
The company discloses contracted revenue allocated to unsatisfied or partially unsatisfied performance obligations, including $190.564 billion total RPO at December 31, 2025.
GE Aerospace provides RPO timing detail, expecting 86% of services-related RPO to be recognized within 15 years and 89% of equipment-related RPO within 5 years.
The proxy reports operating metrics such as $66.2 billion of 2025 orders, approximately $190 billion of backlog, 1,450 LEAP-1A durability kits delivered, and 26% higher engine deliveries.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance has a positive independence marker, with the 2026 proxy stating that 8 of 9 director nominees are independent. The packet does not provide committee structure, insider ownership, or compensation-plan design, so the governance view is constructive but incomplete for a Rule #1 investor.
points: As of January 29, 2026, H. Lawrence Culp, Jr. served as Chairman and CEO, combining the board chair and chief executive roles in one named leader.
The 2026 proxy states that 8 of 9 director nominees are independent, providing a concrete counterweight to the combined Chairman and CEO structure.
Governance oversight must account for business exposures disclosed in the filings, including 60% non-U.S. revenue in 2025 and U.S. Government sales equal to 10% of revenue.
The provided facts do not state executive compensation structure, director committee assignments, insider ownership levels, or specific shareholder-rights provisions.

types.type: Installed engine base and aftermarket services
types.summary: GE Aerospace has a large recurring-service base tied to approximately 50,000 commercial engines across widebody and narrowbody platforms. Commercial Engines & Services generated $25.010 billion of 2025 services revenue versus $8.304 billion of equipment revenue, showing that the installed base is a major driver of revenue and segment profit.

The business has unusually visible contracted demand, with approximately $190 billion of backlog and $190.564 billion of remaining performance obligations at December 31, 2025. Services-related RPO was $163.029 billion, and GE expects to recognize 86% of that services RPO within 15 years, supporting long-duration aftermarket visibility.

GE Aerospace's military installed base is approximately 30,000 engines and powers a variety of defense platforms globally, including two-thirds of all U.S. military combat and helicopter fleets. Defense & Propulsion Technologies generated $10.554 billion of 2025 revenue, and 2025 wins included a $5 billion USAF contract for F110 engines.

GE Aerospace participates in major engine programs through joint ventures that expand its product reach. The proxy states that equipment includes products made by CFM, a 50/50 joint venture with Safran Aircraft Engines, and Engine Alliance, a 50/50 joint venture with Pratt & Whitney.

GE Aerospace describes FLIGHT DECK as its proprietary lean operating model, focused on safety, quality, delivery, and cost in that order. The proxy links FLIGHT DECK to more than 40% higher material input from priority suppliers year over year and a 26% increase in total engine deliveries in 2025.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: GE Vernova is led by Scott Strazik, who signed the 2025 Form 10-K as Chief Executive Officer, President, and Director, with Kenneth Parks as Chief Financial Officer and Matthew Potvin as Controller and Chief Accounting Officer. The CEO is also the chief operating decision maker for Power, Wind, and Electrification, which makes segment execution central to judging leadership quality.
points: Scott Strazik is both CEO and chief operating decision maker, so reported results in Power, Wind, and Electrification are directly tied to the top leadership structure.
Management's stated strategy is specific: grow the installed base, improve underwriting, streamline toward core workhorse products, use Lean, invest with third parties, and return cash.
Execution is mixed by segment: Power produced $2.902 billion of 2025 segment EBITDA, while Wind reported a $598 million segment EBITDA loss.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Capital allocation is active and explicitly tied to cash generation, with a stated plan to return at least one-third of cash generation after investments. In 2025, the company returned $3.6 billion through repurchases and dividends, but it also committed capital to M&A and restructuring, so investors should track whether these uses improve segment returns.
points: GE Vernova returned $3.6 billion to shareholders in 2025 through share repurchases and dividends, then doubled its annual dividend to $2 per share in December.
The company increased its buyback authorization from $6 billion to $10 billion, giving management more room to return capital when cash generation supports it.
GE Vernova acquired the remaining 50% of Prolec GE for $5.3 billion, with the proxy stating the transaction closed in February 2026.
A July 2025 restructuring plan is expected to cost about $225 million to $250 million and produce estimated savings of about $250 million beginning in 2026.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The strongest alignment evidence in the packet is the company's stated capital-return policy and the large 2025 cash return to shareholders. Compensation infrastructure exists, including long-term incentives and clawback policy, but the packet does not provide incentive metrics or executive ownership levels, limiting how much alignment can be graded from these facts alone.
points: The proxy states a policy of returning at least one-third of cash generation to stockholders after investments, connecting shareholder returns to internally generated cash.
The 2025 capital return of $3.6 billion was backed by $4.987 billion of cash from operating activities and $3.710 billion of free cash flow.
The Form 10-K exhibit list includes a 2024 Long-Term Incentive Plan, an annual incentive plan, executive severance plans, change-in-control severance policy, and clawback policy.
The packet does not provide executive ownership levels or incentive-plan performance weights, so investors cannot fully judge whether pay is tightly linked to per-share value creation.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: GE Vernova's filings provide useful operating detail beyond consolidated revenue, including segment revenue, business-unit revenue, segment EBITDA, backlog, service mix, and cash-flow measures. The disclosure lets investors separate the profitable Power and Electrification segments from the loss-making Wind segment and connect backlog to remaining performance obligations.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K reports segment revenue and EBITDA, including Power revenue of $19.767 billion and EBITDA of $2.902 billion versus Wind's $598 million EBITDA loss.
Business-unit revenue is also disclosed, such as Gas Power at $16.006 billion, Onshore Wind at $8.241 billion, and Grid Solutions at $6.620 billion in 2025.
The proxy reports $59 billion of 2025 orders and $150 billion of year-end backlog, defined on a remaining performance obligation basis.
Backlog disclosure includes segment service percentages: Power backlog was 74% services, Wind was 58% services, and Electrification was 12% services.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance has a clearly independent board structure, with Steve Angel as independent non-executive Chair and Scott Strazik as the only non-independent director. The packet also shows formal controls, compensation, and risk disclosures, but the board is new, with all nine directors having less than three years of tenure.
points: The 2026 proxy lists Steve Angel as independent non-executive Chair, 8 independent directors, and Scott Strazik as the only non-independent director.
The board profile includes 3 female directors, 3 Black or Latino directors, and all 9 directors with less than 3 years of tenure.
Management concluded internal control over financial reporting and disclosure controls were effective as of December 31, 2025, and Deloitte audited internal control effectiveness.

Governance oversight must include legacy risks: environmental and worker exposure reserves were $135 million, and asset retirement obligations were $541 million, including nuclear decommissioning obligations.

types.summary: GE Vernova states that its installed base generates approximately 25% of the world's electricity, creating a large foundation for recurring service work. In 2025, the company reported $17.098 billion of services revenue, and Power's $94.4 billion backlog was 74% services.

types.type: Scale and portfolio breadth in power infrastructure
types.summary: GE Vernova reported $38.068 billion of 2025 revenue across Power, Wind, and Electrification, spanning Gas Power, Nuclear Power, Hydro Power, Steam Power, Grid Solutions, Power Conversion & Storage, and Electrification Software. This breadth gives the company exposure to electricity generation, transfer, conversion, storage, and orchestration rather than a single equipment category.

The company reported $59 billion of 2025 orders and $150 billion of year-end backlog on a remaining performance obligation basis. Backlog is spread across Power at $94.4 billion, Wind at $21.6 billion, and Electrification at $34.7 billion, giving investors concrete visibility into long-cycle customer commitments.

GE Vernova's 2025 revenue was geographically split between $17.341 billion from the U.S. and $20.728 billion from non-U.S. regions, including Europe, Asia, the Americas outside the U.S., and the Middle East and Africa. That spread reduces reliance on one domestic market, though the packet does not quantify local share positions.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Alphabet's leadership is anchored by Sundar Pichai, who is both CEO, director, and the Chief Operating Decision Maker for segment resource allocation. The operating record in the packet shows a large, profitable core in Google Services and a rapidly improving Google Cloud segment, while Other Bets remains a meaningful drag on operating income.
points: Sundar Pichai is identified as Chief Operating Decision Maker and uses segment operating income or loss in the annual planning process to allocate resources and assess performance.
The 2025 Form 10-K was signed by CEO Sundar Pichai, CFO Anat Ashkenazi, and Principal Accounting Officer Amie Thuener O'Toole, identifying clear executive accountability for reporting.
Google Services produced $139.404 billion of 2025 operating income, while Google Cloud operating income rose to $13.910 billion from $1.716 billion in 2023.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Alphabet's capital allocation is heavily shaped by the cash-generating Google Services business and large reinvestment in AI, cloud, and other long-duration initiatives. The company generated $101.746 billion of operating cash flow in FY2025, but Other Bets lost $7.515 billion and Alphabet-level activities reduced operating income by $16.760 billion.
points: Management evaluates segments using operating income or loss against the annual plan, which ties resource allocation to the reported economics of Google Services, Google Cloud, and Other Bets.
Alphabet spent $45.427 billion on research and development in FY2025, and Alphabet-level costs included certain AI-focused shared research and development activities.
Google Cloud revenue increased to $58.705 billion in 2025 from $33.088 billion in 2023, while its operating income increased to $13.910 billion over the same period.
Other Bets generated only $1.537 billion of 2025 revenue and reported a $7.515 billion operating loss, making it the clearest capital allocation risk in the packet.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The packet shows several shareholder-alignment safeguards, including independent board leadership, fully independent key committees, and a clawback provision in the 2021 Stock Plan. The main offset is the 2026 proposal to add 200,000,000 Class C shares to the equity plan reserve, which shareholders should evaluate for dilution and incentive discipline.
points: Alphabet's 2026 proxy states that the Board has an independent Chair separate from the CEO role, reducing reliance on a combined chair-and-CEO governance structure.
The Audit Committee, Risk and Compliance Committee, Leadership Development, Inclusion and Compensation Committee, and Nominating and Corporate Governance Committee are each 100% independent.
The 2021 Stock Plan includes a recoupment or clawback provision allowing Alphabet to recover compensation to the extent permitted or required by law, policy, or exchange rules.
The annual meeting agenda includes a board-recommended amendment to increase the Class C share reserve under the 2021 Stock Plan by 200,000,000 shares.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Alphabet gives investors segment-level reporting for Google Services, Google Cloud, and Other Bets, including multi-year revenue and operating income or loss. The packet also includes disclosure of operating cash flow, R&D expense, balance sheet items, long-lived assets by geography, and how the CEO uses segment operating income in resource allocation.
points: The 2025 10-K reports Google Services, Google Cloud, and Other Bets separately, with 2023, 2024, and 2025 revenue and operating income or loss for each segment.
Alphabet discloses the business contents of each segment, including ads, Android, Chrome, Maps, Play, Search, YouTube, Cloud Platform, Workspace, and Other Bets services.
The filing explains that Sundar Pichai monitors actual segment results versus the annual plan and uses segment operating income or loss for annual resource allocation.
The packet includes SEC company facts for FY2025 operating cash flow, research and development expense, stockholders' equity, long-term debt, and cash and equivalents.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Alphabet's governance structure includes 10 director nominees, 70% independence, an independent Chair, and fully independent key committees. The Board also added a Risk and Compliance Committee in October 2025, which is relevant given disclosed regulatory and operational risks, including tax examinations, foreign tax assessments, fines, and settlements.
points: The 2026 proxy lists 10 director nominees: Larry Page, Sergey Brin, Sundar Pichai, John L. Hennessy, Frances H. Arnold, Marty Chávez, John Doerr, Roger Ferguson Jr., K. Ram Shriram, and Robin Washington.
Alphabet states that 70% of director nominees are independent, and the Board has an independent Chair separate from CEO Sundar Pichai.
The Board established a Risk and Compliance Committee in October 2025 to focus on evolving regulatory and operational risks.
Alphabet disclosed $11.512 billion of gross unrecognized tax benefits, IRS examination of 2019 through 2021 returns, and foreign tax assessments involving transfer pricing or permanent establishment.

types.type: Scale economics across services, cloud, and R&D
types.summary: Alphabet's scale is visible in $402.836 billion of 2025 revenue, $129.039 billion of operating income, and $101.746 billion of operating cash flow. That scale supports $45.427 billion of FY2025 R&D expense and Alphabet-level AI-focused shared research activities, creating reinvestment capacity that smaller competitors would find difficult to match from internal cash flow.

Google Services combines ads, Android, Chrome, devices, Google Maps, Google Play, Search, and YouTube in one reporting segment. The segment produced $342.721 billion of 2025 revenue and $139.404 billion of operating income, funded by advertising, YouTube subscriptions, NFL Sunday Ticket, Google One, app sales, in-app purchases, and devices.

Google Cloud includes infrastructure and platform services, applications, Google Cloud Platform, Google Workspace, and other enterprise services sold through consumption-based fees and subscriptions. Revenue rose to $58.705 billion in 2025 from $33.088 billion in 2023, while operating income improved to $13.910 billion from $1.716 billion.

types.type: Asset base supporting global technical infrastructure
types.summary: Alphabet reported $261.818 billion of long-lived assets as of December 31, 2025, including $195.337 billion in the United States and $66.481 billion internationally. The size of this asset base supports businesses such as Google Services and Google Cloud, both of which depend on large-scale technical infrastructure to deliver advertising, consumer, and enterprise services.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Goldman Sachs has clear named executive accountability, with David Solomon serving as Director, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, Denis P. Coleman III as Chief Financial Officer, and Sheara J. Fredman as Chief Accounting Officer. The leadership team is running the firm around Global Banking & Markets and Asset & Wealth Management while also managing a notable consumer retreat.
points: The 2025 Form 10-K signatures identify David Solomon, Denis P. Coleman III, and Sheara J. Fredman in the top CEO, CFO, and accounting roles.
The proxy frames management's agenda around "One Goldman Sachs," world-class durable businesses, and operating at scale across Global Banking & Markets and Asset & Wealth Management.
The Apple Card transition and prior GreenSky and seller-financing loan portfolio sales show management is reducing consumer-related exposure rather than expanding that strategy.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Goldman Sachs generated $58.283 billion of 2025 net revenues, $17.176 billion of net earnings, and 15.0% ROE on average common shareholders' equity of $108.726 billion. Capital allocation looks focused on core institutional and wealth businesses, but investors should track deposit funding, loan mix, and the remaining consumer credit exposure.
points: For 2025, the firm reported $21.852 billion of pre-tax earnings, $16.300 billion of net earnings applicable to common shareholders, and EPS of $51.32.
Average total shareholders' equity was $123.733 billion, including $15.007 billion of preferred stock, with average tangible common shareholders' equity of $101.954 billion.
Average interest-bearing deposits rose to $466.888 billion in 2025 from $428.934 billion in 2024, while the average rate on total deposits fell to 3.94% from 4.73%.
Gross loans were $239.882 billion at December 2025, led by other collateralized loans, commercial real estate, residential real estate, corporate loans, and credit card loans.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Goldman Sachs provides some direct alignment data through security-holder approved equity plans and disclosed common shareholder earnings metrics. The packet does not provide buyback or dividend figures, so shareholder alignment is best judged here through equity compensation structure, earnings per share, ROE, and board-shareholder engagement.
points: As of December 31, 2025, Goldman Sachs had 15,420,097 shares issuable under outstanding RSUs and no outstanding options under equity compensation plans.
The firm had 53,871,082 shares available for future issuance under equity compensation plans, and no equity compensation plans not approved by security holders.
The Lead Director engaged with shareholders representing over 35% of shares outstanding over the prior year, often alongside Compensation Committee Chair Kimberley Harris.
The proxy reports net earnings applicable to common shareholders of $16.300 billion and EPS of $51.32, giving investors direct common-shareholder performance measures.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Goldman Sachs' filings provide useful investor detail on business lines, strategic objectives, capital metrics, deposit funding, loan composition, and credit performance. The disclosures include exact ROE and ROTE definitions, balance-sheet components, allowance movements, consumer charge-offs, and trading-plan activity rather than relying only on broad narrative.
points: The proxy identifies the operating focus as Global Banking & Markets and Asset & Wealth Management, and lists the three strategic objectives management is pursuing.
The proxy explains ROTE by defining tangible common shareholders' equity as total shareholders' equity less preferred stock, goodwill, and identifiable intangible assets.
The 10-K discloses average deposit balances, deposit rates, uninsured deposits in U.S. and non-U.S. offices, loan categories, loan tenors, and floating-rate exposure.
Credit reporting includes total net charge-offs of $1.250 billion, a 0.6% total net charge-off ratio, and separate consumer and wholesale net charge-off ratios.
The 10-K states disclosure controls were effective at year-end 2025 and reports no director or executive officer trading-plan entries, modifications, or terminations during the quarter.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance activity appears high based on the disclosed board meeting and engagement record, including 71 Board and committee meetings in 2025 and extensive Lead Director and committee chair activity. The board also has specific issues to oversee, including consumer exit execution, uninsured deposits, loan credit performance, and compensation-related shareholder engagement.
points: The 2026 proxy lists 13 director nominees and states that Lakshmi Mittal will retire from the Board after nearly 18 years of service.
The Board and its committees held 71 meetings in 2025, while the Lead Director held over 125 additional meetings and engagements.
Other committee chairs collectively had over 200 additional meetings and engagements, indicating substantial board-level activity beyond formal Board and committee meetings.
Shareholder engagement included the Lead Director and, often, Compensation Committee Chair Kimberley Harris speaking with holders representing over 35% of shares outstanding.
Disclosed oversight topics include $120.30 billion of uninsured U.S. deposits, $79.79 billion of uninsured non-U.S. deposits, and consumer net charge-offs of 6.1%.

types.type: Operating scale in securities and asset businesses
types.summary: Goldman Sachs operates at large scale for a securities broker, dealer, and flotation company, with 2025 net revenues of $58.283 billion and pre-tax earnings of $21.852 billion. Its disclosed operating focus spans Global Banking & Markets and Asset & Wealth Management, but the packet does not provide market share or client retention data to support a stronger moat grade.

The firm had average interest-bearing deposits of $466.888 billion in 2025, up from $428.934 billion in 2024, and the average rate on total deposits declined to 3.94% from 4.73%. That funding base supports a large balance sheet, but $120.30 billion of uninsured U.S. deposits and $79.79 billion of uninsured non-U.S. deposits keep the advantage from looking unqualified.

Goldman Sachs reported $239.882 billion of gross loans at December 2025, including other collateralized loans, commercial real estate, residential real estate, corporate loans, and credit card loans. This lending capacity can support broad client activity across the firm, but the packet also shows credit sensitivity, including a 6.1% consumer net charge-off ratio in 2025.

Goldman Sachs reported FY2025 cash and equivalents of $182.092 billion and stockholders' equity of $116.905 billion, alongside average total shareholders' equity of $123.733 billion. Those figures indicate substantial financial capacity, though operating cash flow was negative $12.587 billion for FY2025, so this is not graded as a strong standalone moat.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Home Depot has named leaders tied directly to its current priorities in core retail, online experience, technology, and Pro growth. Edward P. Decker combines the Chair, President, and CEO roles, while CFO Richard V. McPhail and executives overseeing customer experience, online, information technology, and Pro markets support a strategy centered on interconnected retail and Pro capabilities.
points: Edward P. Decker has served as President and Chief Executive Officer since March 2022 and Chair since October 2022, giving one leader control over strategy and board leadership.

Richard V. McPhail has been Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer since September 2019, providing continuity through major capital allocation, acquisition, and systems-transformation decisions.
Jordan Broggi leads Customer Experience and Online, Angela Brown leads information technology, and Michael Rowe leads Pro, matching executive roles to the company's stated growth priorities.
Home Depot emphasizes internal talent development: more than 90% of U.S. store leaders began as hourly associates, and field leadership received increased equity awards in Fiscal 2025.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Home Depot's capital allocation framework is explicit: reinvest to grow faster than the market, pay a quarterly dividend, and return excess cash through repurchases after reinvestment and dividends. Fiscal 2025 shows both reinvestment and shareholder returns, with $3.7 billion of capital expenditures and $9.2 billion returned through cash dividends.
points: The company's stated priorities put business reinvestment first, followed by quarterly dividends, with share repurchases only after reinvestment needs and dividends are addressed.
In Fiscal 2025, Home Depot invested $3.7 billion in capital expenditures while reporting $164.7 billion in sales, $20.9 billion in operating income, and 25.7% ROIC.
Home Depot increased its quarterly cash dividend 2.2%, from $2.25 to $2.30 per share, and says it has paid dividends for 156 consecutive quarters.
Acquisition spending is aimed at the Pro market: SRS goodwill was tied to roofing, delivery network capabilities, sales force growth, and additional addressable market opportunities.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Home Depot returns substantial cash to shareholders and uses equity awards below the senior-executive level, including store managers and assistant store managers. Alignment is tempered by elevated leverage and acquisition integration complexity, with FY2025 long-term debt of $49.397 billion and recent SRS and GMS transactions expanding the business mix.
points: Fiscal 2025 shareholder returns included $9.2 billion of cash dividends, supported by $21.172 billion of operating cash flow reported in the SEC company facts summary.
Field leadership received increased equity awards in Fiscal 2025, tying store managers and assistant store managers more directly to company performance and shareholder value creation.
The SRS acquisition added $18.028 billion of net assets acquired, including $5.4 billion of customer-relationship intangibles with a 20-year weighted average useful life.
Balance-sheet risk matters for owners: FY2025 long-term debt was $49.397 billion, compared with $1.659 billion of cash and equivalents and $1.044 billion of stockholders' equity.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Home Depot provides investors with SEC filings, a proxy investor factsheet, XBRL company facts, and 10-K detail on products, acquisitions, internal controls, and financial scale. The disclosures give concrete segment and category information, acquisition accounting for SRS and GMS, and year-over-year comparisons for sales, operating income, and ROIC.
points: The proxy investor factsheet reports Fiscal 2025 sales of $164.7 billion, operating income of $20.9 billion, net earnings of $14.2 billion, and ROIC of 25.7%.
The same factsheet compares Fiscal 2025 with Fiscal 2024, showing sales rose from $159.5 billion while operating income fell from $21.5 billion and ROIC fell from 31.3%.
The 10-K identifies the primary segment's major product lines as Building Materials, Décor, and Hardlines, and lists categories such as Appliances, Lumber, Paint, Plumbing, and Flooring.
Acquisition disclosure is detailed: SRS assets acquired, liabilities assumed, customer-relationship intangibles, trade names, and post-acquisition sales contribution are all separately described.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance disclosures show formal board and shareholder processes, auditor oversight, internal-control reporting, and several active shareholder issues. Investors should note the combined Chair and CEO role, the 2026 proposal on an independent board chair, acquisition-related internal-control exclusions for GMS, and named shareholder proposals covering data privacy, plastics, biodiversity, healthcare access, and charitable-support discrimination.
points: For the 2026 annual meeting, shareholders are asked to elect 12 directors, ratify KPMG as independent auditor, approve Say-on-Pay, and consider officer exculpation and charter amendments.

The proxy lists seven shareholder proposals, including customer data privacy risks, plastics, biodiversity, associate healthcare access, charitable-support discrimination, and an independent board chair proposal.
Management concluded disclosure controls and internal control over financial reporting were effective as of February 1, 2026, and KPMG issued an effective internal-control opinion.
GMS was excluded from the February 1, 2026 internal-control assessment because it was acquired during fiscal 2025, representing about 3% of consolidated assets excluding goodwill and intangibles.

types.summary: Home Depot operates at unusual scale for home-improvement retail, with Fiscal 2025 sales of $164.7 billion and primary product lines of Building Materials, Décor, and Hardlines. Its category breadth spans Appliances, Bath, Electrical, Flooring, Hardware, Lumber, Outdoor Garden, Paint, Plumbing, Power, and Storage Organization, making the assortment difficult to replicate in one format.

Home Depot's strategy specifically targets growth in sales to Pros through a unique ecosystem of capabilities, and recent acquisitions deepen that focus. SRS was acquired for Pro-market growth, roofing exposure, delivery network capabilities, and sales force expansion; GMS was added to accelerate a multi-category building materials distribution vision.

types.type: Interconnected store, digital, and fulfillment experience
types.summary: Home Depot identifies a best-in-class interconnected customer experience as a core strategic pillar and says it is investing across online, in-store, and fulfillment experiences. The company has dedicated leadership for Customer Experience and Online, information technology, and Pro, indicating that digital, store, and fulfillment capabilities are managed as strategic operating priorities.

types.type: Real estate, supply chain, and operating network
types.summary: Home Depot's shareholder letter names scale, real estate portfolio, supply chain network, and relationships with Pros as competitive advantages. The SRS acquisition also adds enhanced delivery network capabilities, while Fiscal 2025 operating cash flow of $21.172 billion provides funding capacity for continued investment in associates, customer capabilities, and fulfillment.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: IBM is led by Arvind Krishna, who holds the combined roles of Chairman, President, and Chief Executive Officer, with James J. Kavanaugh serving as Senior Vice President, Finance and Operations, and Chief Financial Officer. The leadership team is managing a large, multi-segment enterprise technology company with FY2025 revenue of $61.86 billion across Software, Consulting, Infrastructure, and Financing.
points: Arvind Krishna combines board and executive leadership as Chairman, President, and CEO, which gives IBM a clear central decision-maker but also concentrates leadership authority in one role.

James J. Kavanaugh holds the CFO role and also oversees Finance and Operations, a relevant structure for a company with $13.931 billion of FY2025 operating cash flow and $49.884 billion of long-term debt.

IBM's leadership is responsible for distinct operating segments: Software, Consulting, Infrastructure, and Financing, each with separate business lines such as Hybrid Cloud, Automation, Transaction Processing, and Infrastructure Support.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: IBM's capital allocation is anchored by large internal investment, acquisition activity, and balance-sheet management. FY2025 research and development expense was $6.775 billion, while the company also carried $49.884 billion of long-term debt and $13.947 billion of cash and cash equivalents at year-end.
points: IBM spent $6.775 billion on research and development in FY2025, a meaningful internal investment supporting software, infrastructure, and technology lines such as Hybrid Cloud, Automation, Data, and Transaction Processing.

The company continued acquisition activity, including the HashiCorp acquisition on February 27, 2025, and prior StreamSets and WebMethods acquisition-related disclosures referenced in the 2025 filing.

IBM's year-end balance sheet included $49.884 billion of long-term debt against $27.307 billion of stockholders' equity, making leverage an important capital allocation constraint for investors to watch.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: IBM's proxy describes a year-round stockholder engagement process, with feedback used across AI, corporate governance, board composition, and executive compensation. The packet also shows substantial equity compensation plan capacity, including 32,163,174 equity awards outstanding or available and 40,998,234 securities remaining available for future issuance at year-end 2025.
points: The 2026 proxy says IBM uses year-round stockholder engagement feedback to inform practices and policies in AI, governance, board composition, and executive compensation.
Equity compensation is material: IBM reported 32,163,174 equity awards outstanding or available under plans and 40,998,234 securities remaining available for future issuance at December 31, 2025.
IBM's 2025 filing references acquisition-specific compensation plans, including the IBM HashiCorp Acquisition Long-Term Performance Plan adopted in connection with the HashiCorp acquisition.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: IBM provides investors with detailed segment and business-line disclosure in its annual filing, breaking operations into Software, Consulting, Infrastructure, and Financing. The filing also discloses remaining performance obligations, equity compensation plan capacity, credit loss allowances, and revenue-based provisions, giving investors concrete data to track contracted revenue, financing risk, and dilution capacity.
points: The 2025 10-K breaks Software into Hybrid Cloud, Automation, Data, and Transaction Processing, which helps investors separate software exposure rather than viewing IBM as one broad technology business.
IBM separately reports Consulting lines such as Strategy and Technology and Intelligent Operations, and Infrastructure lines such as Hybrid Infrastructure and Infrastructure Support.
The filing includes remaining performance obligation disclosure for periods one and two, indicating contracted future revenue is important enough to be separately tracked.

IBM reports financing-related credit metrics, including allowance for credit losses rising from $273 million to $276 million and revenue-based provisions rising from $298 million to $388 million in 2025.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: IBM's Board actively oversees enterprise risk, including cybersecurity, talent management, and responsible business initiatives, according to the 2026 proxy. Governance is mixed: the Board added Ramon Laguarta since the last Annual Meeting and maintains shareholder engagement, but the Chairman and CEO roles are combined under Arvind Krishna.
points: The Board's stated enterprise risk oversight includes cybersecurity, talent management, and responsible business initiatives, all relevant risks for a large enterprise technology company.
IBM added Ramon Laguarta to the Board since the last Annual Meeting, showing at least one recent board refreshment action disclosed in the 2026 proxy.
Arvind Krishna serves as both Chairman and CEO, so investors should recognize that board leadership and executive leadership are combined rather than separated.

The proxy says stockholder feedback informs corporate governance, board composition, and executive compensation, tying engagement topics directly to governance practices rather than only investor relations messaging.

types.type: Switching costs from enterprise systems and contracted work
types.summary: IBM's business includes Transaction Processing, Infrastructure Support, Consulting, Financing Services, and Used Equipment Sales, all categories that can be embedded in enterprise operations. The 2025 filing also discloses remaining performance obligations for periods one and two, showing contracted future revenue is material enough for separate tracking.

IBM operates at large scale, with FY2025 revenue of $61.86 billion, operating cash flow of $13.931 billion, and research and development expense of $6.775 billion. That spending base supports multiple technology lines, including Hybrid Cloud, Automation, Data, Transaction Processing, Hybrid Infrastructure, and Infrastructure Support.

types.type: Breadth across software, consulting, infrastructure, and financing
types.summary: IBM's operating model spans Software, Consulting, Infrastructure, and Financing, rather than relying on a single product category. Its disclosed lines include Hybrid Cloud, Automation, Data, Strategy and Technology, Intelligent Operations, Hybrid Infrastructure, Infrastructure Support, Financing Services, and Used Equipment Sales.

IBM's 2025 filing references acquisition-related operating segments and acquisition-specific compensation plans, including the HashiCorp acquisition completed on February 27, 2025. It also references StreamSets and WebMethods acquisition disclosures, indicating acquisitions are part of how IBM adds capabilities to its enterprise technology portfolio.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Intel is operating under refreshed leadership, with Lip-Bu Tan appointed Chief Executive Officer in 2025 and also serving as a director. The 2025 Form 10-K was signed by Tan, CFO David Zinsner, and Chief Accounting Officer Scott Gawel, while the board added four independent directors since 2024.
points: Lip-Bu Tan became CEO in 2025, which makes current execution partly a transition story rather than a long, proven management record under the same leader.
The 2025 Form 10-K identifies David Zinsner as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer and Scott Gawel as Corporate Vice President and Chief Accounting Officer.
Board refreshment is active: Intel added four independent directors since 2024, including Dr. Craig H. Barratt in November 2025.
Management is executing restructuring plans aimed at lowering expenses, reducing management layers, and reallocating resources toward Intel's core client and server businesses.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Intel still has large financial resources, with FY2025 revenue of $54.228 billion, operating cash flow of $11.471 billion, and stockholders' equity of $99.270 billion. Capital allocation is under pressure from heavy R&D spending, long-term debt of $46.282 billion, and restructuring plans intended to reduce operating expenses, capital expenditures, and cost of sales.
points: Intel spent $16.046 billion on research and development in FY2025, showing that advanced semiconductor technology remains a major claim on cash generation.
The 2024 Restructuring Plan targets lower operating expenses, lower capital expenditures, and lower cost of sales while continuing to fund Intel's core strategy.
The 2025 Restructuring Plan shifts resources toward core client and server businesses by reducing investment in lower-priority programs and initiatives.
Intel reported $8.249 billion of cash and equivalents against $46.282 billion of long-term debt, making balance-sheet discipline important for future flexibility.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Intel's proxy describes several shareholder-friendly voting rights, including one vote per share, no dual-class share structure, no poison pill, and annual director elections by majority vote with a resignation policy. Executive compensation is designed around financial and operational performance, strategic objectives, pay-for-performance, governance, and stockholder feedback, but the packet does not provide outcome-level pay results.
points: Shareholders have proxy access at 3% ownership held for 3 years for up to 20% of the board, according to Intel's 2026 proxy.
Stockholders can call special meetings at a 15% ownership threshold, and Intel reports no poison pill or dual-class share structure.
The Compensation Committee states that executive pay is tied to financial and operational performance, strategic objectives, pay-for-performance, and stockholder feedback.
Intel's 2006 Equity Incentive Plan granted equity awards to more than 73% of employees in 2025, extending stock-based incentives broadly across the workforce.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Intel provides detailed SEC reporting across financial concepts, operating segments, customers, geography, and governance practices. The packet cites FY2025 revenue, net income, operating cash flow, R&D, debt, cash, and equity from SEC company facts, plus 10-K XBRL tags for segment, customer-concentration, and geographic revenue categories.
points: Intel's SEC company facts report FY2025 revenue of $54.228 billion, net income of $1.689 billion, and operating cash flow of $11.471 billion.
The 2025 Form 10-K XBRL segment tags identify Client Computing Group, Data Center and AI, Network and Edge, Intel Foundry, Mobileye, Altera, and All Other Segments.
Customer-concentration disclosures identify Dell Inc., Lenovo Group Limited, HP Inc., and Intel's three largest customers as tied to FY2025 net sales revenue disclosures.
Geographic revenue categories in the 2025 10-K include the United States, China including Hong Kong, Singapore, Taiwan, and other countries.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Intel's governance framework includes an independent board chair, executive sessions of independent directors at least four times annually, annual director elections, proxy access, and one vote per share. Board oversight explicitly covers strategy and business, human capital management, enterprise risk management, ethics, legal and regulatory matters, cybersecurity, and corporate social responsibility.
points: The board has an independent chair, and independent directors meet in executive session at least four times annually, according to Intel's 2026 proxy.
The board oversees strategy and business, human capital management, enterprise risk management, ethics, legal and regulatory matters, cybersecurity, and corporate social responsibility.
Director elections are annual and decided by majority vote with a resignation policy, giving shareholders a regular mechanism to vote on board accountability.
Legal oversight matters because Intel had accrued approximately $1.0 billion for VLSI litigation as of December 27, 2025, with possible excess losses not reasonably estimable.

types.summary: Intel has company-developed technologies that can support differentiation in advanced chips and foundry services. The 2025 Form 10-K describes EMIB 2.5D packaging for high-density interconnect of heterogeneous chips, PowerVia backside power delivery for advanced nodes, and RibbonFET gate-all-around transistor technology using ribbon-shaped nanosheets.

Intel's size gives it capacity to fund very large technical programs, with FY2025 revenue of $54.228 billion, operating cash flow of $11.471 billion, and R&D expense of $16.046 billion. This scale is meaningful, but the moat is tempered by $46.282 billion of long-term debt and ongoing restructuring to reduce expenses and capital intensity.

Intel participates across multiple semiconductor categories rather than relying on one product line. Its 2025 reporting categories include Client Computing Group, Data Center and AI, Network and Edge, Intel Foundry, Mobileye, Altera, and All Other Segments, while Altera offers programmable semiconductors, primarily FPGAs, for a broad range of applications.

Intel sells into major global computer and technology channels, with FY2025 customer-concentration tags identifying Dell Inc., Lenovo Group Limited, HP Inc., and Intel's three largest customers. This reach is valuable for volume and market access, but the same disclosure also highlights concentration risk rather than proving customer lock-in.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Johnson & Johnson is led by named executives with clear public accountability in the 2025 Form 10-K: Joaquin Duato as Chairman and CEO, Joseph J. Wolk as CFO, and R. J. Decker Jr. as Controller and Chief Accounting Officer. The leadership team is managing a large healthcare portfolio focused on Oncology, Immunology, Neuroscience, Cardiovascular, Surgery, and Vision.
points: Joaquin Duato signed the 2025 Form 10-K as both Chairman of the Board and Chief Executive Officer, placing operating leadership and board chair responsibilities with one executive.
Joseph J. Wolk signed as Chief Financial Officer, while R. J. Decker Jr. signed as Controller and Chief Accounting Officer, identifying financial reporting responsibility at senior levels.
The proxy frames growth around six businesses: Oncology, Immunology, Neuroscience, Cardiovascular, Surgery, and Vision, giving investors a clear view of management's strategic focus.
Management is also pursuing a separation of the Orthopaedics business, which the Lead Independent Director said is expected to improve focus and shift toward higher-growth markets.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Johnson & Johnson has substantial capital allocation capacity, supported by FY2025 revenue of $85.159 billion, net income of $35.153 billion, operating cash flow of $22.791 billion, and about $20 billion of free cash flow as defined in the proxy. Management combines shareholder returns with innovation investment and portfolio reshaping, including 63 years of dividend increases and the planned Orthopaedics separation.
points: The company reported approximately $20 billion in 2025 free cash flow, defined as operating cash flow less additions to property, plant and equipment.
Johnson & Johnson reported 63 years of dividend increases, showing a long record of returning cash to shareholders through regular dividend growth.
The proxy says the company invested approximately $32 billion in research and inorganic innovation, tying capital allocation to both internal development and external opportunities.
The balance sheet included $24.105 billion of cash and equivalents and $30.651 billion of long-term debt for FY2025, giving investors concrete leverage and liquidity context.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Executive pay is explicitly linked to operating and shareholder outcomes through base salary, annual incentives, and long-term incentives. For named executive officers, performance share units are 60% of the long-term incentive mix and use a three-year performance period tied to cumulative adjusted operational EPS and relative total shareholder return.
points: Annual incentive payouts are based on business and individual performance, which links a portion of executive compensation to operating execution rather than only fixed pay.
Long-term incentives are designed to motivate long-term operational goals, total shareholder return, share price growth, and executive retention, according to the 2026 proxy.
For named executive officers, performance share units represent 60% of the long-term incentive mix and measure three-year cumulative adjusted operational EPS and relative TSR.
The company reported no equity compensation plans not approved by security holders, and all listed outstanding options and rights were under shareholder-approved plans.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Johnson & Johnson provides detailed SEC reporting on financial results, controls, equity compensation, litigation reserves, and strategic priorities. The packet includes FY2025 revenue, net income, operating cash flow, debt, cash, R&D expense, product-platform scale, regulatory approvals, clinical readouts, Phase 3 starts, launches, and active clinical trials.
points: The company filed FY2025 revenue of $85.159 billion, net income of $35.153 billion, and operating cash flow of $22.791 billion in SEC company facts.
The 2026 proxy disclosed 28 Innovative Medicine products and MedTech platforms with more than $1 billion in annual sales, split between 15 products and 13 platforms.
Innovation reporting included 51 Innovative Medicine regulatory approvals, 40+ MedTech regulatory approvals, 32 regulatory submissions, 17 positive key-study readouts, and 11 new Phase 3 programs.
The 2025 Form 10-K disclosed a talc reserve present value of approximately $3.4 billion and stated management could not estimate losses beyond accrued amounts.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance has several investor-friendly structures, including a mostly independent board, a Lead Independent Director, shareholder-approved equity plans, audited internal controls, and disclosure-control certifications by Joaquin Duato and Joseph J. Wolk. The grade is held to middle because investors must also weigh major named risks, including talc litigation, patent challenges, regulatory uncertainty, manufacturing delays, product safety concerns, and Orthopaedics separation execution.
points: The 2026 proxy listed 12 director nominees, stated all nominees were independent except Chairman and CEO Joaquin Duato, and reported average tenure of about five and a half years.
Marillyn A. Hewson served as Lead Independent Director, and independent directors extended her eligibility to serve two years beyond the company's retirement age.
Management concluded internal control over financial reporting was effective as of December 28, 2025, using the COSO 2013 framework, with PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP auditing effectiveness.
Joaquin Duato and Joseph J. Wolk participated in evaluating disclosure controls and procedures and concluded they were effective as of the 2025 Form 10-K period end.

Governance oversight must account for significant risks named in filings, including adverse litigation or government action, healthcare cost containment, patent expirations, and product safety concerns.

types.summary: Johnson & Johnson's portfolio has unusual breadth across Innovative Medicine and MedTech, with 28 products and platforms exceeding $1 billion in annual sales. Those include 15 Innovative Medicine products and 13 MedTech platforms, reducing dependence on a single product family while keeping the business concentrated in large healthcare categories.

The company's moat is supported by a large innovation pipeline across regulated healthcare markets. In 2025, Johnson & Johnson reported 51 Innovative Medicine regulatory approvals in major markets, 40+ MedTech regulatory approvals, 32 regulatory submissions, 17 positive key-study readouts, 11 new Phase 3 programs, 15 launches, and more than 60 active clinical trials.

Johnson & Johnson's FY2025 operating cash flow of $22.791 billion and approximately $20 billion of free cash flow give it substantial capacity to fund research, acquisitions, dividends, and portfolio actions. The proxy also reported approximately $32 billion invested in research and inorganic innovation, reinforcing the company's ability to replenish its healthcare portfolio.

types.type: Diversification across priority therapeutic and procedure categories
types.summary: The proxy identifies six key businesses driving growth: Oncology, Immunology, Neuroscience, Cardiovascular, Surgery, and Vision. This mix gives Johnson & Johnson exposure to both Innovative Medicine and MedTech markets, though filings also name risks from clinical success, regulatory approvals, patent challenges, manufacturing delays, and commercial success of new and existing products.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: JPMorgan Chase has clearly identified senior leadership in its 2025 Form 10-K, with James Dimon signing as Chairman, CEO, and Principal Executive Officer. The leadership structure is supported by named finance and accounting executives and by a Board process that actively reviews succession across senior management.
points: James Dimon signed the 2025 Form 10-K as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, while Jeremy Barnum signed as Chief Financial Officer and Principal Financial Officer.
Elena Korablina signed as Firmwide Controller and Principal Accounting Officer, giving investors named accountability for the company's accounting and financial reporting functions.
The full Board, rather than one person or one committee, is involved in CEO succession planning and assessing senior executives for CEO and Operating Committee potential.
Stephen B. Burke serves as Lead Independent Director, and directors met with more than 100 senior managers during the year to assess the leadership pipeline.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: The packet shows a very large, profitable banking franchise with 2025 managed revenue of $185.6 billion, net income of $57.0 billion, ROE of 17%, and ROTCE of 20%. Board oversight explicitly includes key investments, strategy, investment discipline, shareholder returns, and capital strength.
points: JPMorgan Chase reported FY2025 revenue of $158.104 billion under the SEC Revenues concept and $49.552 billion of net income under NetIncomeLoss.
On a managed basis, the proxy reports 2025 revenue of $185.6 billion, net income of $57.0 billion, return on equity of 17%, and ROTCE of 20%.
The Board meets at least eight times each year and oversees key investments and strategy, with stated focus on investment discipline, shareholder returns, and capital strength.
The 10-K states that JPMorgan Chase acquired certain assets and assumed certain liabilities of First Republic Bank from the FDIC on May 1, 2023.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Shareholder alignment is supported by reported per-share book value data, high managed-basis returns, and stated Board focus on shareholder returns. The packet does not provide executive compensation design, so pay-based alignment cannot be assessed from these facts alone.
points: At December 31, 2025, the proxy reported book value per share of $126.99 and tangible book value per share of $107.56.
The proxy reports common stockholders' equity of $342.393 billion and tangible common equity of $290.018 billion, giving investors concrete capital-base measures to track.
The Board's stated oversight priorities include shareholder returns and capital strength, tying governance attention to financial outcomes relevant to long-term owners.
JPMorgan Chase's By-laws provide proxy access for eligible holders of at least 3% of outstanding shares for at least three consecutive years.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: JPMorgan Chase provides detailed public-company reporting through SEC submissions, the 2025 Form 10-K, and the 2026 proxy. The packet includes GAAP figures, managed-basis performance, a reconciliation for fully taxable-equivalent adjustments, segment definitions, and business-line descriptions across banking, markets, payments, mortgages, and wealth management.
points: The proxy reconciles 2025 reported total net revenue of $182.447 billion plus $3.134 billion of fully taxable-equivalent adjustments to managed-basis revenue of $185.581 billion.
The 2025 Form 10-K glossary defines CCB as Consumer & Community Banking, CIB as Commercial & Investment Bank, AWM as Asset & Wealth Management, and LOB as line of business.

The company describes Markets as CIB's Fixed Income Markets and Equity Markets businesses, with macro activities including Rates, Currencies and Emerging Markets, Fixed Income Financing, and Commodities.

SEC company facts provide standardized FY2025 figures including $158.104 billion of revenue, $49.552 billion of net income, $12.974 billion of operating cash flow, and $327.878 billion of stockholders' equity.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: Governance facts show an active Board structure with a Lead Independent Director, separate independent-director meetings, regular Board meetings, and direct access to senior management. The packet also documents proxy access rights and Board involvement in CEO succession rather than concentrating that responsibility in one person or committee.
points: Stephen B. Burke is identified as Lead Independent Director, and independent directors meet separately at every regular Board meeting for candid discussion and oversight.
The Board meets at least eight times each year, has open access to senior management, and oversees key investments, strategy, investment discipline, shareholder returns, and capital strength.
CEO succession planning is handled by the entire Board, which develops and assesses senior executives who may become CEO or Operating Committee candidates.
Proxy access allows a shareholder group of up to 20 holders with at least 3% ownership for three consecutive years to nominate qualifying Board candidates.

types.summary: JPMorgan Chase operates at a scale that few financial institutions can match, with 2025 managed revenue of $185.6 billion, managed net income of $57.0 billion, and common stockholders' equity of $342.393 billion at year-end. Its disclosed business lines span Consumer & Community Banking, Commercial & Investment Bank, and Asset & Wealth Management, reducing dependence on one activity.

The company has a concrete payments footprint through Merchant Services and Commercial Card. Merchant Services enables merchants of all sizes to accept credit and debit card payments and multiple-currency payments, while Commercial Card serves corporate and public sector clients worldwide with procurement, travel, expense management, and business-to-business payment solutions.

JPMorgan Chase's CIB Markets business includes Fixed Income Markets and Equity Markets, with macro activities in Rates, Currencies and Emerging Markets, Fixed Income Financing, and Commodities. This breadth supports client-driven market-making revenue, though the disclosed revenue drivers include bid-offer spreads and realized and unrealized gains and losses, making the moat more cyclical than contractual.

The company serves clients through multiple lending channels and products, including retail and correspondent mortgage origination and AWM custom lending for Global Private Bank clients. The custom lending definition includes loans to private investment funds and loans collateralized by nontraditional assets such as art work and aircraft, showing specialized relationship-based capabilities.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: KLA is led by Richard P. Wallace as President, Chief Executive Officer and Director, with Bren D. Higgins as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer and Virendra A. Kirloskar as Chief Accounting Officer signing the 2025 10-K. The team runs a large process-control business with $12.156 billion of fiscal 2025 revenue, $3.387 billion of net income, and $3.670 billion of operating cash flow.
points: Richard P. Wallace signed the 2025 10-K as both CEO and Director, tying the named operating leader directly to board-level accountability in the filing.
KLA's leadership allocated $1.297 billion to research and development in fiscal 2025, important for a business centered on wafer inspection, patterning, and semiconductor process control tools.
Management also handled restructuring charges of $7.7 million in fiscal 2025 after $21.6 million in fiscal 2024, tied mainly to the former PCB and Display operating segment.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: KLA combines high cash generation with continued investment in R&D, dividends, and liquidity management. Fiscal 2025 operating cash flow was $3.670 billion, while the company reported $1.977 billion of cash and equivalents, $5.880 billion of long-term debt, and a new revolving credit agreement maturing in 2030.
points: The Board declared a quarterly cash dividend of $1.90 per share on August 7, 2025, payable September 3, 2025 to holders of record on August 18, 2025.
KLA replaced its prior revolving credit facility on July 3, 2025; the new agreement matures July 3, 2030 and permits a requested $500.0 million aggregate increase.
Capital spending needs appear asset-light relative to revenue scale: KLA reported $1.253 billion of net land, property and equipment versus $12.156 billion of fiscal 2025 revenue.
R&D spending of $1.297 billion in fiscal 2025 shows capital is being directed back into the product engine behind Wafer Inspection, Patterning, and related process-control offerings.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: The packet supports several alignment mechanisms, including dividends, an advisory compensation vote, and incentive plans using short-term and long-term awards. It does not provide insider ownership levels or detailed performance targets, so alignment looks structured but cannot be judged as exceptional from the supplied facts alone.
points: KLA returned cash through a declared quarterly dividend of $1.90 per share, giving shareholders a direct capital return tied to board action.
The 2025 proxy asked shareholders to approve named executive officer compensation on a non-binding advisory basis, giving investors a formal say-on-pay channel.

Executive compensation materials include base salary, a short-term executive incentive bonus plan, long-term incentives, and restricted stock unit awards with performance-vesting and service-vesting forms.
Because the packet does not provide executive ownership, director ownership, or the specific incentive metrics, shareholder alignment should be viewed as present but not fully assessable.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: KLA's filings give investors useful operating detail by product category, geography, and segment. The 2025 10-K discloses major revenue lines such as Wafer Inspection, Patterning, Services, and Other, while also explaining revenue recognition for product and service obligations.
points: The 2025 10-K breaks out Wafer Inspection revenue at $6.199 billion, Patterning at $2.196 billion, Services at $2.683 billion, and Other at $204.6 million.

Segment disclosures identify Semiconductor Process Control, Specialty Semiconductor Process, and PCB and Component Inspection, with Wafer Inspection and Patterning linked to Semiconductor Process Control.
Geographic disclosures show China at $4.043 billion, or 33% of fiscal 2025 revenue, and Taiwan at $3.205 billion, or 27%, based on ship-to location.
The 10-K explains that product revenue is generally recognized when control transfers, while service revenue is recognized ratably over the service period or when performed.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: KLA has identifiable board and audit structures in the packet, including ten director nominees and an external audit opinion on internal control over financial reporting. Governance also has risks investors should watch, including customer concentration, geographic concentration, debt, and restructuring tied to the former PCB and Display operating segment.
points: For the 2025 annual meeting, the Board recommended ten director nominees: Robert Calderoni, Jason Conley, Tracy Embree, Jeneanne Hanley, Kevin Kennedy, Michael McMullen, Victor Peng, Jamie Samath, Susan Taylor, and Richard Wallace.
Management concluded disclosure controls and internal control over financial reporting were effective as of June 30, 2025, and PwC also opined internal control was effective.
PricewaterhouseCoopers LLP has served as KLA's auditor since 1977, giving investors a long audit relationship to consider when evaluating oversight and independence questions.
Governance oversight must address concentration risks: one customer represented about 19% of fiscal 2025 revenue, while China and Taiwan together represented 60% by ship-to location.

types.summary: KLA's business is concentrated in specialized semiconductor process-control categories rather than broad electronics equipment. In fiscal 2025, Wafer Inspection generated $6.199 billion, or 51% of total revenue, and Patterning generated $2.196 billion, or 18%, both offered in the Semiconductor Process Control segment.

KLA has the revenue and cash generation to fund a large technical development budget. Fiscal 2025 revenue was $12.156 billion, operating cash flow was $3.670 billion, and research and development expense was $1.297 billion, supporting continued investment in inspection, patterning, and related process-control tools.

KLA earns meaningful revenue after initial system sales through Services and lifecycle offerings. Services generated $2.683 billion, or 22% of fiscal 2025 revenue, while Other revenue included refurbished systems, remanufactured legacy systems, and enhancements and upgrades for previous-generation Semiconductor Process Control products.

types.type: Customer and geographic embeddedness with concentration risk
types.summary: KLA ships into major semiconductor manufacturing regions, with China contributing $4.043 billion, or 33% of fiscal 2025 revenue, and Taiwan contributing $3.205 billion, or 27%. This suggests deep exposure to key chip-production geographies, but the moat is tempered by concentration because one customer accounted for about 19% of total revenue.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Coca-Cola is led by James Quincey, identified in the 2025 10-K as Chairman and Chief Executive Officer, with John Murphy as President and Chief Financial Officer. The 2026 proxy describes an active CEO succession, with Quincey preparing to pass leadership to Henrique Braun, who joined Coca-Cola 30 years earlier.
points: James Quincey's 2026 proxy letter frames the company as a total beverage company with about 200 focused brands, not just the owner of one iconic drink.
The leadership transition is named and specific: Quincey said this was his last CEO letter and that Henrique Braun was being prepared as successor.
The executive officer group identified in the 2025 10-K includes John Murphy as President and CFO and Erin L. May as Controller and Chief Accounting Officer.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Coca-Cola generates large cash flow, with FY2025 revenue of $45.754 billion, net income of $10.714 billion, and operating cash flow of $11.599 billion reported in SEC company facts. The packet also shows significant capital needs and obligations, including $2.112 billion of 2025 property, plant and equipment purchases and $36.377 billion of long-term debt reported for FY2023.
points: The 2026 proxy Annex C reports 2025 free cash flow of $5.296 billion after $2.112 billion of purchases of property, plant and equipment.
Annex C also reports free cash flow excluding the fairlife contingent consideration payment of $11.365 billion, making that payment a notable item for investors to separate.
The company reported $9.366 billion of cash and equivalents and $24.856 billion of stockholders' equity for FY2025 in SEC company facts.
The provided packet does not include dividends, share repurchases, acquisition prices, or return-on-invested-capital data, so capital return discipline cannot be fully judged here.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Coca-Cola's compensation program ties annual cash incentives to predefined performance measures and caps any participant's annual payment at $10 million. Long-term incentive tools include stock options, PSUs, restricted stock, RSUs, performance-based awards, SARs, and other stock-based awards, but the packet does not provide ownership levels or realized pay outcomes.
points: The Annual Incentive Plan uses predefined performance measures, which gives shareholders a stated link between annual cash pay and company performance goals.
No participant in the Annual Incentive Plan may receive more than $10 million for an annual payment, according to the 2026 proxy.
Long-term incentive plans may use multiple equity-linked vehicles, including stock options, PSUs, restricted stock, RSUs, SARs, and performance-based awards.
The 2026 proxy states no named executive officer received RSUs or a performance cash award in 2025, an important detail for evaluating that year's pay mix.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Coca-Cola provides investors with standard SEC reporting plus detailed proxy disclosures on strategy, succession, compensation, and non-GAAP cash flow reconciliation. The 2025 10-K iXBRL text identifies reporting contexts for geographic segments, concentrate and finished product operations, and major bottling-related equity method investees.
points: The 2025 10-K identifies reporting contexts for Europe, Middle East & Africa, Latin America, North America, Asia Pacific, and Bottling Investments.
The 10-K iXBRL text includes contexts for both concentrate operations and finished product operations, including U.S. and non-U.S. member tags.
The 2026 proxy Annex C reconciles operating cash flow, capital expenditures, free cash flow, and free cash flow excluding the fairlife contingent consideration payment.
The proxy letter gives investors specific operating context, including 2.2 billion daily servings, more than 200 countries and territories, 32 billion-dollar brands, and about 200 total brands.

sections.title: Governance
sections.summary: The 2026 proxy card lists 12 director nominees and the Board recommended voting for all of them, for advisory approval of executive compensation, and for ratifying Ernst & Young LLP as independent auditor for fiscal 2026. Governance disclosures also show board-level handling of shareholder proposals on sustainability, plastics packaging, DEI, ingredients, and sustainability disclosure.
points: The 12 director nominees are Herb Allen, Bela Bajaria, Ana Botín, Henrique Braun, Christopher C. Davis, Carolyn Everson, Thomas S. Gayner, Max Levchin, Amity Millhiser, James Quincey, Caroline J. Tsay, and David B. Weinberg.
James Quincey is both Chairman and Chief Executive Officer in the 2025 10-K signatures, while Henrique Braun appears as a 2026 director nominee and planned CEO successor.
The 2025 10-K exhibit list includes Coca-Cola's Incentive Based Compensation Recoupment Policy as Exhibit 97, incorporated from the 2023 Form 10-K.
The Board recommended votes against shareholder proposals seeking additional action or reports on sustainability governance, plastics packaging, DEI, ingredients, and sustainability disclosure.

types.summary: Coca-Cola's moat is anchored in a broad beverage portfolio with 32 billion-dollar brands and approximately 200 focused brands. The 2026 proxy says its drinks are enjoyed 2.2 billion times per day across more than 200 countries and territories, giving the company unusually broad consumer reach for a beverage business.

Coca-Cola operates through a networked organization that combines global scale with local market expertise, supported by bottling partners investing for growth. The 2025 10-K references major equity method or investee relationships including Coca-Cola Europacific Partners, Coca-Cola FEMSA, Coca-Cola HBC, Coca-Cola Bottlers Japan, Coca-Cola Icecek, Andina, and Monster.

The company has continued to develop large beverage brands, with James Quincey's 2026 proxy letter stating that Coca-Cola added 12 billion-dollar brands since 2017. The same letter says marketing has become digital-first and consumer-centric, with digital representing a majority of media investment in 2025.

The 2025 10-K iXBRL text includes reporting contexts for both concentrate operations and finished product operations, including U.S. and non-U.S. member tags. This structure matters because Coca-Cola is not only a brand owner; it also reports across operating models and regions tied to its global beverage system.

sections.title: Leadership
sections.summary: Lilly's leadership is managing a large, fast-growing pharmaceutical business with a clear focus on new medicines, manufacturing scale, and global access. The 2025 filing was signed by David Ricks as Chair, President, and CEO, alongside CFO Lucas Montarce and Chief Accounting Officer Donald Zakrowski, giving investors named accountability for financial reporting and execution.
points: David Ricks signed the 2025 10-K as Chair, President, and Chief Executive Officer, while Lucas Montarce served as Executive Vice President and Chief Financial Officer.

Management describes Lilly's model as discovering first- or best-in-class molecules, developing them faster than competitors, scaling through medicines and manufacturing capacity, and reinvesting profits into innovation.
In 2025, Lilly advanced orforglipron, its oral GLP-1 therapy, with submissions in the U.S. and Japan for obesity and in the EU for obesity and type 2 diabetes.
The company also reported progress on drug approvals for cancer and digestive diseases, plus FDA label or regimen updates for Kisunla and Omvoh.

sections.title: Capital Allocation
sections.summary: Capital allocation is heavily aimed at R&D, manufacturing capacity, and selective business development. In 2025, Lilly reported $65.179 billion of revenue, $20.640 billion of net income, large early- and late-stage R&D spending, and major manufacturing expansion plans tied to oral medicines and broader supply capacity.
points: Lilly disclosed 2025 early-stage R&D of $4.881 billion, primarily discovery through Phase 2 trials, and late-stage R&D of $8.456 billion, primarily Phase 3 trials.
The company reported 2025 expenditures for long-lived assets of $8.672 billion, including property, equipment, and computer software costs.
Lilly announced manufacturing expansions including a planned $3 billion Netherlands facility, more than $1.2 billion in Puerto Rico, and a $5 billion manufacturing facility in Virginia.
Business development included the 2025 acquisition of Verve Therapeutics, a Boston-based clinical-stage company developing genetic medicines for cardiovascular disease.

sections.title: Shareholder Alignment
sections.summary: Lilly's stated shareholder alignment comes mainly through reinvesting profits into medicines, R&D, manufacturing, and future innovation rather than through disclosed capital-return policies in the packet. Compensation alignment is mixed: annual cash bonus calculations used adjusted EPS, but the adjustments removed several items that can materially affect reported earnings.
points: The company says it aims to reinvest profits into the next wave of innovation, which fits its 2025 spending on R&D, manufacturing capacity, and acquired in-process R&D.
For 2025 annual cash bonuses, the Talent and Compensation Committee adjusted reported EPS from $22.95 to $27.06 after excluding specified items such as acquired IPR&D charges.

The excluded bonus-calculation items also included amortization of intangible assets, asset impairments and restructuring, tax-law-change impacts, divestiture-related impacts, and foreign exchange rate impacts.
As of December 31, 2025, Lilly had 42,396,427 securities available for future issuance under approved equity plans and 3,484,792 shares underlying outstanding equity awards.

sections.title: Communication
sections.summary: Lilly gives investors useful operating detail through SEC filings, even though it reports only one reportable segment.
